title: Error budget forecasting & dashboard templates
preview:
Purpose:
This working doc proposes a lightweight forecasting approach for error budgets that ties SLO erosion to operational cost and trace signal quality. The goal is a set of metric definitions, a minimal trace sampling strategy for forecast inputs, and two dashboard templates (operational & executive) that surface predictive risk windows and remediation runbooks.

Context and motivation:
- We have strong SLI definitions for 99p latency and success-rate, but lack forward-looking visibility: teams discover budget burn only after incidents start.
- Forecasting SLO burn gives teams a runway to act (scale, rollback, reduce batch size, or enable fallback models) before customer impact.
- Forecasts must be cheap to compute and rely on surface telemetry already available in the runtime + a small set of targeted trace samples.

Audience / stakeholders:
- Primary: serving runtime oncall & platform owners (pager recipients).
- Secondary: product and GTM (exec dashboard for weekly forecasting), capacity planning, SRE.

High-level approach:
1) Define a compact set of predictive metrics (5–8 series) that correlate with short-term SLO degradation.
2) Use an exponential weighted moving average (EWMA) + short-horizon trend fit (12–30 min window) to project error budget burn in the next 1–6 hours.
3) Feed a trace-sampling signal (low-cost flags described below) to detect mode shifts (e.g., KV cache misses, model fallback if any).
4) Surface results on two dashboards: "Operational forecast" and "Executive runway" with drilldowns to traces and remediation checklist.

Predictive metric definitions (canonical names + short definitions):
- request.count_total: total inference requests received (per min). Use as normalization and burst detector.
- error.rate_user_per_min: percentage of requests returning client-visible errors (500s, malformed outputs). (use 1m window)
- tail.latency.p99_1m: p99 latency in 1m buckets for token-generation path (includes model/network).
- kv_cache.miss_rate: fraction of requests that missed the prefix/KV cache (explicit cache miss tag).
- model.fallback_rate: fraction of requests routed to fallback/cheaper-variant models (if routing exists).
- infra.queue_depth: per-worker request queue length (avg over 30s).
- trace.anomaly_index: heuristic score (0..1) derived from trace feature flags (see trace sampling).

Forecasting algorithm (lightweight):
- Windowing: produce 1m aggregated series over last 60m, compute EWMA with alpha=0.2 for smoothing and linear trend on last 12 points (12 min).
- Burn rate proxy: convert tail.latency.p99 and error.rate_user_per_min into a unified burn-rate by normalizing against SLO thresholds:
    burn_component_latency = max(0, (p99 - slo_p99) / slo_p99)
    burn_component_error = error_rate / slo_error_budget_unit
    burn_rate = 0.6*burn_component_error + 0.4*burn_component_latency + 0.15*kv_cache.miss_rate
- Forecast: extrapolate burn_rate trend forward 1h and 6h, compute remaining budget at current burn and at forecasted burn. Flag windows where budget <= 20% remaining.

Trace sampling & instrumentation guidance (minimal-cost, high-signal):
- Triggered sampling: only collect full traces when one of these occurs in a 1m window: p99 > 1.5x slo_p99, kv_cache.miss_rate spike > x3 baseline, or model.fallback_rate > 5%. Default collect rate otherwise: 0.1% of requests.
- Lightweight trace markers: instrument a short span at request start and at model-kv boundary with the following fields: request.id, route, model_variant, kv_hit (boolean), batching_size, client-tier. These are small and cheap but enable quick group-by breakdowns.
- Anomaly index: compute a simple feature vector per-minute from sampled traces (avg batch_size, kv_hit_frac, model_variant_entropy) and normalize to 0..1; expose as trace.anomaly_index time series.

Operational dashboard (detailed, for oncall):
- Layout: 3-column canvas
  Col A (Top): Forecast widget (burn-rate timeline) showing: current burn, 1h & 6h projected remaining budget bands, highlighted alert windows.
  Col A (Bottom): Incoming request volume & queue_depth heatmap (per-region).
  Col B: SLI panels: p50/p95/p99, error.rate_user_per_min, request.success_rate.
  Col C: Signal breakdown: kv_cache.miss_rate, model.fallback_rate, trace.anomaly_index, and a small table of top 10 routes contributing to burn.
- Drilldown behavior: click on a flagged window in Forecast widget filters trace view (last 20 sampled traces) and opens runbook link with recommended mitigations.
- Queries: use existing metrics ingestion labels: service=runtime, env=prod, region, model_variant. Examples (promql-like pseudo):
  - p99: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(latency_bucket[1m])) by (le, route))
  - kv miss rate: sum(rate(kv_miss_total[1m])) / sum(rate(kv_request_total[1m]))
  - forecast burn: query derived in pipeline and exposed as metric forecast.error_budget_rate_1m

Executive runway dashboard (summary for product/execs):
- Single view: a 7-day burn forecast (daily granularity) + current week remaining budget percent.
- Cost-impact overlay: estimated additional GPU-hours if scale-up required to avoid SLO erosion (model: per 10% projected increase in requests, estimate X vGPU-hours). Keep numbers conservative and annotated with assumptions.
- Weekly action items: auto-populated from remediations taken (scale/rollback/optimize) and their effect on the forecast in the following 24–48 hours.

Alerting and runbooks:
- Primary alert: forecast.error_budget_window_1h — triggers when projected remaining budget < 20% in next 60 minutes. Severity: P1 (page oncall).
- Secondary alert: trace.anomaly_index_spike — triggers when anomaly index increases > 3x baseline for 3 consecutive minutes. Severity: P2 (pager for platform lead).
- Runbooks: for each alert provide checklist: check queue_depth, check recent deploys (link to release), identify top routes, temporarily enable fallback model or increase capacity, toggle cache warmup, and post incident note.

Operational costs & sampling budget:
- Forecasting pipeline should compute forecast metrics in a separate low-cost pipeline (e.g., batch job every 1m) to avoid adding ingestion pressure to ops pipeline.
- Trace sampling budget: cap full traces to 1500 traces/hour per region unless a triggered spike is active. This keeps storage and egress bounded.

Implementation phasing (MVP -> 3 quarters):
- MVP (4 weeks): implement forecast pipeline using existing aggregated metrics, deploy Operational dashboard with static runbook links, add primary alert. Keep trace sampling rules passive (0.1% default).
- Phase 2 (8–12 weeks): add triggered sampling & anomaly index computation, integrate trace drilldowns, add executive dashboard with cost overlay.
- Phase 3 (ongoing): tighten models, back-test forecasts on historical incidents, add auto-mitigation playbooks for safe actions (scale/burst).

Open questions / risks:
- How to validate the burn-rate weighting coefficients (latency vs error vs cache miss)? Propose backtesting against last 12 months of incidents.
- Cost estimates in exec view are model-dependent; need guardrails so execs don’t over-index on them.
- Sampling could miss rare systemic failures if thresholds are tuned poorly — require periodic review.

Next steps / owners:
- Priya: prototype 1m forecast pipeline using last-30d data (owner: Priya).
- Evan: dashboard canvas & PromQL equivalents for the SRE Grafana team.
- Sofia: review trace fields and align instrumentation on model-serving repo.
- Deck: prepare a short 10-slide review for SRE+Product in 3 weeks.

Notes to reviewers (quick):
- This is a working proposal; prefer conservative sampling and clear opt-out for noisy routes.
- Feedback wanted on burn-rate formula, acceptable forecast horizon (1h vs 6h), and cost-estimate assumptions.

---
Revision history:
- 2024-11-05 created (first-pass definitions)
- 2025-01-09 added trace sampling caps and anomaly_index idea
- 2025-03-12 tightened dashboard layout and added implementation phasing

title: Quant + Batch KPI Simulator — APAC stress test
preview:
Purpose:
This sheet is a working simulator for APAC regional capacity under mixed quantization and batching profiles. Focus is on: expected throughput uplift, SLO tail-latency risk, and an estimate of end-user quality degradation (regression budget). Intended for infra planning and to feed Dedicated capacity commit recommendations for H1 2025 APAC expansion.

High-level notes / assumptions:
- Workload: mixed chat + completion + embedding bursts; 65% short interactions (<=128 tokens), 25% mid (129-512), 10% long (>512).
- Traffic profile (simulated): morning peak 07:00–10:00 JST, secondary peak 19:00–22:00 JST. Peak CBPM (concurrent backlogged model requests) used in sim: 18k at peak.
- GPU pool mix (target APAC region): H100-80GB x40, A100-80GB x60, L4 x200 (inference-optimized small instances).
- Models considered: rw-large v2 (32B), rw-medium v1 (7B), open-compat small (2.7B). Baseline perf measured at FP16/no-quant on H100.
- Quantization profiles: FP16 (baseline), Q4_K_M (aggressive), INT8 (experimental), Q5_K_S (balanced).
- Batch strategies: dynamic batching (auto with max latency budget), static microbatch sizes tested {1,2,4,8,16,32}.
- KV/prefix cache hit scenarios: 0%, 20%, 50% (cache hit reduces tokens served and latency significantly).

Simulator matrix (excerpt):
Columns: GPU | Model | Quant | Batch | TP tokens/sec (per GPU) | 95p latency ms (seq=128) | quality delta (relative eval) | cost delta ($/1M tokens)
H100 | rw-large-32B | FP16 | 1 | 4200 | 42 | 0.0% | +0.00
H100 | rw-large-32B | Q4_K_M | 8 | 19600 | 78 | -1.8% | -62.5
H100 | rw-large-32B | INT8 | 8 | 23500 | 110 | -3.6% | -72.0
H100 | rw-large-32B | Q5_K_S | 4 | 14400 | 56 | -0.9% | -45.0
A100 | rw-large-32B | FP16 | 1 | 3600 | 48 | 0.0% | +0.00
A100 | rw-large-32B | Q4_K_M | 8 | 15800 | 105 | -2.0% | -58.0
L4   | rw-medium-7B  | Q4_K_M | 16 | 41000 | 220 | -2.5% | -90.0
L4   | rw-medium-7B  | Q5_K_S | 8  | 21000 | 140 | -1.0% | -50.0

Interpretation notes:
- Raw tokens/sec numbers are synthetic and derived from microbenchmarks collected during Jan–Feb 2025 internal runs. They include kernel-level throughput adjustments and an empirically derived model for memory-copy overhead.
- Aggressive quant + large batching yields highest tokens/sec but increases 95p latency (tail) and larger quality delta. Use only behind canary or for non-SLO critical embeddings.
- L4s are very cost-effective for 7B-class models with higher batch sizes but show severe latency spikes for chat workloads without prefix caching.

Scenario summaries (sim runs):
1) Baseline (no quant, conservative batching): total provisioned: H100 x20, A100 x30, L4 x120. Expected usable throughput at peak: ~11.4M tokens/min. 95p latency target (<=150ms seq=128) met. Cost baseline = $X (see cost tab). Quality delta = 0 by definition.
2) Aggressive optimization (max quant + large batch on H100/A100, move 40% low-SLA traffic to L4): Provisioned: H100 x12, A100 x20, L4 x180. Expected throughput: ~18.2M tokens/min. 95p latency exceeded in short-chat tail without caching; predicted user-facing regression risk = medium. Cost reduction = ~32% vs baseline.
3) Balanced (Q5 on larger models, Q4 on 7B, conservative batching for chat paths): Provisioned: H100 x16, A100 x28, L4 x150. Throughput: ~14.8M tokens/min. 95p latency within SLO for chat when prefix cache hit >=20%. Quality delta: -0.8% avg on eval sets. Cost reduction = ~18%.
4) Cache-heavy (50% prefix cache hit + Q4 on long completions): allows very aggressive batching on long tails; recommended for scheduled batch-generation workloads and nightly offline jobs. Peak user latency improved significantly.

Risk table (quick):
- Risk: Silent quality regressions; Mitigation: shard small prompt-set evals into canary for every model/quant rollout, block if >1.0% regression on regression-set.
- Risk: Tail latency spikes under sudden P50->P95 traffic skew; Mitigation: keep runway of 10% spare H100 CP capacity and fallback routing to non-quant FP16 variant if tail exceeds 95p target for 5 consecutive minutes.
- Risk: Billing surprise from L4 burst usage; Mitigation: per-customer rate-limits and quota thresholds at RDSE layer.

Quick sensitivity (what changes throughput most):
- Batch size (for large tokens) gives superlinear gains up to GPU memory limits.
- Quantization level (Q4 vs Q5 vs INT8) yields the largest steady-state token/sec delta but increases regression risk.
- Cache hit rate has multiplicative effect — 20% -> ~14% effective throughput uplift; 50% -> ~40% uplift.

Small table: recommended conservative default profiles (for Hosted API production):
- Chat/default route: Q5_K_S on rw-large, max_batch=8, dynamic_latency_budget=120ms -> priority: keep quality.
- Rerank/embedding: Q4_K_M on rw-medium/rw-small, max_batch=32, allow fallback_window=200ms -> priority: throughput/cost.
- Background generation (batch): INT8/Q4 aggressive, static_batch=64, relaxed SLOs -> priority: maximize throughput.

Cost estimator snapshot (high-level):
- Baseline cost per 1M tokens: $X. Aggressive quant scenario reduces cost/1M by ~25–40 depending on L4 mix.
- Note: numbers exclude network egress and storage cache costs.

Open questions / TODOs (notes-to-self):
- Run a 72h APAC soak with 50% of production traffic routed to quantized-canary (INFRA-2143).
- Add real user-eval (A/B) for quality delta across 10 representative flows (search-summaries, code-gen, casual chat). Owner: Ethan.
- Formalize fallback policy integration with Router service (PR referenced above).
- Get procurement estimate for additional H100 units if balanced scenario selected (Finance contact: Marcos).

Next steps (immediate):
- Convert this sheet into a formal capacity request: include the scenario table above + cost delta and expected SLA impact. Draft due 2025-03-12.
- Schedule canary run and wire up monitoring dashboards: latency by route, token/sec by GPU-type, quality regression feeds (evals) into Optimize.

Appendix (raw CSV export lines for ingestion into simulator):
TIMESTAMP,SCENARIO,GPU,MODEL,QUANT,BATCH,TP_TOKS_SEC,95P_LAT_MS,QUALITY_DELTA,COST_DELTA_PER_1M
2025-02-10,baseline,H100,rw-large-32B,FP16,1,4200,42,0.0,0.0
2025-02-10,baseline,A100,rw-large-32B,FP16,1,3600,48,0.0,0.0
2025-02-12,aggr,H100,rw-large-32B,Q4_K_M,8,19600,78,-1.8,-62.5
2025-02-12,aggr,L4,rw-medium-7B,Q4_K_M,16,41000,220,-2.5,-90.0

Notes on provenance: many figures above come from Jan 2025 perf runs in /benchmarks/quant-batch/apac/ and local microbench folder. This is explicitly a working draft; numbers will be refined after the 72h soak.

Contact: Priya Natarajan (pri@redwood.ai) — use #infra-capacity for quick sync.

--- end sheet ---

title: Private deployment security playbook — RBAC v2, audit schema, SIEM + network hardening
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook consolidates network and controller-level hardening patterns for Redwood Private deployments with a focus on RBAC v2, standardized audit log schema for downstream SIEM ingestion, evidence automation (for compliance evidence), and recommended VPC/firewall/mTLS configurations. Use this as the reference for customer on-prem and VPC installs; it is intentionally prescriptive so engineering and deployment teams have deterministic steps to pass security reviews.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: SREs, platform engineers, security reviewers, solutions engineers implementing private deployments.
- Scope: Network topology patterns, firewall rule recommendations, mTLS guidance for inter-service traffic, RBAC v2 binding and policy patterns, audit log schema and SIEM mapping, automated evidence collection checklist.

1) High-level VPC/topology patterns
- Preferred: Multi-subnet staging pattern with explicit control plane subnet and isolated workload subnets per tenant/workspace.
- Minimal layout (single-customer):
  * control-plane-subnet (private, restricted egress to mgmt and logging endpoints)
  * inference-worker-subnet (GPU/accelerator nodes)
  * management-subnet (jumpboxes, Bastion hosts, operator tools)
  * logging-egress-subnet (centralized egress for SIEM/KMS)
- Cross-account/networking: Use VPC peering or Private Service Connect depending on cloud. Default deny at subnet boundary, allow only enumerated flows.

Diagram references:
- Diagram A: VPC-subnet-layout (see linked_artifacts CONFLUENCE-PLAT-9876 for SVG).
- Diagram B: mTLS mesh between control plane and workers, with sidecar certs issued by onsite intermediate CA.

2) Recommended firewall rules (principle: deny-by-default, least privilege)
- Control-plane-subnet inbound:
  * Allow TCP 22 from management-subnet CIDR for operator access (source-limited).
  * Allow control-plane API port 8443 from allowed mgmt IPs and internal load-balancer IPs.
  * Deny everything else.
- Worker-subnet inbound:
  * Allow mTLS port 15012 (example) from control-plane-subnet and load-balancer IPs.
  * Allow healthcheck ports from internal LB range.
  * Block inbound SSH from internet — use bastion in management-subnet.
- Egress rules (strict):
  * Allow egress to KMS endpoints (TLS 443) for key operations.
  * Allow egress to SIEM/Log-collector IPs or proxy on 443.
  * Block outbound to high-risk IP ranges by default (optional curated denylist).
- Implementation notes: Use cloud-native network policies (e.g., Security Groups, GCP Firewall, Calico network policies for K8s overlay) and ensure rule descriptions include JIRA ticket or playbook reference.

3) mTLS and service identity (recommended architecture)
- Model: short-lived leaf certs issued by a local CA or a customer-managed PKI (Vault/ACM/RSA HSM).
- Key points:
  * All intra-cluster RPC and HTTP/2 endpoints must require mTLS.
  * Enforce client cert verification and CN/subject verification mapped to service identities (e.g., spiffe URIs).
  * Use TLS 1.2+ and disable legacy ciphers.
  * Automate rotation: cert lifetime <= 24h for workloads if using short-lived certs; 90 days for node identities.
- Bootstrap flow (simplified):
  1. Node/sidecar authenticates to PKI (via signed CSR over secure channel).
  2. PKI returns short-lived cert and trust bundle.
  3. Service advertises spiffe identity; control plane accepts validated identities through RBAC mapping.

4) RBAC v2 — model and examples
- Design goals: least-privilege bindings, attribute-based role scoping (by workspace/customer), auditable grant records, and automated revocation hooks.
- Concepts:
  * Role: collection of permissions (e.g., generate-token, start-job, read-logs).
  * Principal: user/service account identified by external identity provider or cert CN.
  * Scope: namespace/workspace/customer-id.
  * Binding: maps principal -> role within scope with metadata and expiry.
- Example RBAC v2 binding (human-readable):
  * binding_id: bind-2025-02-09-ops-oncall
  * principal: svc:ops-bot@customers/acme
  * role: inference-runner.control (start/stop jobs)
  * scope: customer:acme:production
  * expiry: 2025-05-01T00:00:00Z
- Best practices:
  * All elevated bindings require justification field and ticket id.
  * Use short-lived bindings for on-call breakout tasks.
  * Maintain a mapping table between external SSO groups and internal roles in Confluence.

5) Audit log schema (canonical) — required fields for evidence and SIEM ingestion
- All audit records MUST include the following top-level fields (canonical names):
  * event_id (string, uuid)
  * timestamp (ISO-8601 UTC)
  * actor_type (\"user\" | \"service\")
  * actor_id (string — SSO user id or service account id)
  * actor_identity_provider (string)
  * action (string, e.g., \"job.create\", \"rbac.binding.create\", \"network.firewall.update\")
  * resource_type (e.g., \"job\", \"binding\", \"node\", \"vpc\")
  * resource_id (string)
  * outcome (\"success\" | \"failure\")
  * ip_address (source IPv4 or IPv6)
  * user_agent (if applicable)
  * request_id (correlate RPC chains)
  * request_payload_hash (sha256 hex) — optional but preferred for non-sensitive indexing
  * change_set (free text or JSON string describing mutation)
  * auth_method (\"mTLS\" | \"OIDC\" | \"API_KEY\" | \"SAML\")
  * trace_id (for distributed tracing correlation)
- Example (single-line JSON for a sample event — include as a literal in logs):
  {
    \"event_id\": \"b3f9d2f6-1a23-4e7b-af6c-123456789abc\",
    \"timestamp\": \"2025-02-27T14:12:33Z\",
    \"actor_type\": \"service\",
    \"actor_id\": \"svc:deployment-controller:infra\",
    \"actor_identity_provider\": \"local-ca\",
    \"action\": \"rbac.binding.create\",
    \"resource_type\": \"binding\",
    \"resource_id\": \"bind-2025-02-27-canary\",
    \"outcome\": \"success\",
    \"ip_address\": \"10.12.5.4\",
    \"request_id\": \"req-7a8b9c\",
    \"auth_method\": \"mTLS\"
  }

6) SIEM export and mapping guidance
- Output format: JSON-lines (ndjson) is the required output for all SIEM exports. Each line is one canonical audit event as above.
- Recommended pipeline: local log shipper -> Filebeat/Fluentd pipeline -> central Aggregation (Log proxy) -> SIEM (Splunk/Datadog/SumoLogic).
- Field mapping examples (Splunk):
  * event_id -> event_id
  * timestamp -> _time
  * actor_id -> user or client_id
  * action -> event_action
  * resource_type/resource_id -> object_type/object_id
  * trace_id/request_id -> correlation_id
- Micro-batching: ensure shipper can buffer (disk) for at least 24h to survive network interruptions. Include HMAC-signed manifest files to validate completeness.

7) Evidence automation (for customer audits)
- Goal: produce a compressed evidence bundle per audit period containing: RBAC binding snapshot, signed audit logs (for the period), firewall config exports, PKI trust chain, and SIEM retention manifest.
- Steps to automate:
  1. Snapshot RBAC bindings via API (/v2/rbac/export?at=2025-02-01T00:00:00Z) -> produce bindings.json and sign with infra-deploy key.
  2. Export canonical audit log window (ndjson), generate SHA256 manifest and sign.
  3. Export firewall rule table (cloud provider API) and attach IDs; run a drift detection check relative to approved baseline.
  4. Collect CA chain and CRL/OCSP evidence, include rotation timestamps.
  5. Package and upload to secure evidence bucket with retention and access audit.
- Automations should be scheduled and triggered by an audit runbook (JIRA-SEC-314). Evidence bundles must be immutable once sealed.

8) Hardening checklist (quick-run, pre-deployment)
- Network:
  * [ ] Subnets created per playbook and default-deny applied.
  * [ ] Security groups/firewalls have descriptive names and ticket references.
  * [ ] Egress restricted to KMS and SIEM endpoints.
- TLS & PKI:
  * [ ] mTLS enforced for all intra-service traffic.
  * [ ] Cipher suites restricted, TLS 1.2+ only.
  * [ ] Cert rotation policy configured and tested.
- RBAC & Auth:
  * [ ] RBAC v2 enabled, binding expiry policies set.
  * [ ] External SSO group mappings verified.
  * [ ] Audit trail for all admin actions exists and exports to local log collector.
- Logging & SIEM:
  * [ ] Audit logs formatted to canonical schema.
  * [ ] Shipper configured with disk buffering and TLS to SIEM.
  * [ ] Log retention and indexing costs estimated and documented.
- Evidence & Compliance:
  * [ ] Evidence automation job scheduled and successful test run completed.
  * [ ] Evidence bundle signing keys available in HSM/KMS.

9) Operational runbooks and escalation
- Incident: suspected RBAC compromise
  * Immediate: revoke suspected bindings (rbac.revoke API) and rotate affected service certs; create high-priority incident in JIRA and notify security-alerts Slack channel.
  * Post-incident: run evidence export for compromise window and deliver to IR team.
- Incident: large-scale log loss
  * Immediate: switch shipper to alternate sink (s3 proxy) and preserve last-known manifest; notify oncall.

10) Appendix — quick reference snippets
- Sample firewall CLI (GCP):
  gcloud compute firewall-rules create deny-infer-from-internet \\
    --direction=INGRESS --action=DENY --rules=tcp:0-65535 \\
    --source-ranges=0.0.0.0/0 --network=redwood-private-net \\
    --description=\"Deny all direct inbound to inference workers; use LB/bastion\"

- Sample audit export command (example):
  curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" \"https://private-controlplane.example/api/v2/audit/export?from=2025-02-01T00:00:00Z&to=2025-02-28T23:59:59Z\" \\
    -o audit-2025-02.ndjson

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Decide final expiry window for workload certs for air-gapped customers (24h vs 72h trade-off).
- Confirm standard SIEM parsers for Datadog vs Splunk and add parser repo to github.com/redwood-inference/security/parsers.
- Add automated compliance checks that validate firewall rules against approved baseline on every deployment commit.

Notes to reviewers: this is a working playbook; please add concrete provider CLI snippets where you have expertise (AWS, Azure, GCP) and tag the PR with your initials.

title: Region-aware SDK flags and integration guide
preview:
Purpose
This note defines recommended SDK surface, runtime flags, and integration patterns for making upstream Redwood clients region-aware. Goal: enable application and platform teams to respect customer region pins and enforce fallback behavior with minimal friction.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: SDK behavior for server-side and backend client libraries (Node, Python, Java). Focus on client-side routing hints, local validation, telemetry, and test hooks.
- Non-goal: changing server routing or enforcement logic (that lives in control plane). This doc defines SDK-side controls and integration checklists only.

Background / problem statement
Customers can pin tenant traffic to a geographic region (e.g., eu-west-1) for compliance. If the SDK always routes to the default hosted endpoint, we lose early enforcement guarantees and observability. SDKs should:
  - surface customer pin expectations to the control plane via headers
  - provide local validation and clear errors when pins block a request
  - support configurable fallback policies for transient capacity issues
  - emit telemetry to help SRE and Customer Success understand pin-related failures

Design principles
1) Opt-in-by-default: Existing apps shouldn’t change behavior unless they explicitly enable region-aware mode.
2) Explicitness: The SDK must make pin-related routing visible (logs, error codes).
3) Fail-fast for violations: If a request conflicts with a tenant pin and customer config forbids fallback, the SDK should fail locally with a structured error.
4) Testable: Provide deterministic hooks for unit/integration tests to simulate pin enforcement and fallbacks.

Proposed SDK surface (high-level)
- Config object additions (pseudocode):
  - regionAware: boolean // opt-in flag
  - tenantRegion: string | null // explicit pin (e.g., "eu-west-1"); when set, SDK adds header X-Redwood-Tenant-Region
  - allowFallback: enum("none","regional-only","any-available") // controls fallback granularity
  - fallbackOrder: list<string> // optional ordered list of fallback regions
  - residencyTimeoutMs: int // how long to wait for regional capacity before triggering fallback
  - telemetryTag: string // optional tag for downstream dashboards

Example config (Node):
const client = new RedwoodClient({
  apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_KEY,
  regionAware: true,
  tenantRegion: process.env.TENANT_REGION, // injected by customer onboarding pipeline
  allowFallback: 'regional-only',
  fallbackOrder: ['eu-west-1','eu-central-1'],
  residencyTimeoutMs: 250,
});

HTTP headers generated by SDK
- X-Redwood-Tenant-Region: <region> (when tenantRegion is set)
- X-Redwood-Allow-Fallback: <none|regional-only|any-available>
- X-Redwood-Fallback-Order: comma-separated-list (optional)
- X-Redwood-Client-Region-Preference: <client-detected-region> (when region detection available)

Local validation / pre-flight checks
1) If regionAware=true and tenantRegion is provided:
   - perform a lightcontrol-plane preflight (cached per-tenant for T minutes) to verify pin is accepted and get enforcement mode (strict | soft). This call is non-blocking for first request if cached state is unknown, but should proceed concurrently with the main request.
2) If enforcement=strict and allowFallback==none: 
   - SDK must reject requests immediately with error code REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION and message explaining remediation steps (include link to support playbook).
3) If enforcement=soft: SDK should attempt regional route and, upon timeout/residencyTimeoutMs, follow allowFallback policy.

Observability and telemetry
- Emit structured events (JSON) to the SDK logger/tracing system:
  - event: pin_check_result { tenant, requested_region, enforcement_mode, cached_age_ms, result: allowed|blocked|fallback, fallback_target }
  - metrics: pin_failure_count, pin_fallback_count, pin_latency_ms (histogram)
- Tag all downstream requests with telemetryTag so Console dashboards can group by onboarding batch/customer.
- Add a sample tracing span name: redwood.pin.check for distributed traces.

Error handling and user-facing messages
- Standardized error code: REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION (http 409 / 403 depending on control plane response)
- User message: "Request not permitted: tenant data residency pinned to <region>. Contact your admin or enable fallback in your client config. See https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/PRIV/Residency+Guide"
- Suggested remediation in error metadata: ["set allowFallback","contact-support","upgrade-plan-for-dedicated"]

Fallback ordering behavior (details)
- Fallback attempt sequence:
  1) Try primary region (tenantRegion) with strict routing attempts until residencyTimeoutMs.
  2) If allowFallback=="regional-only": try fallbackOrder regions in order, then fail.
  3) If allowFallback=="any-available": attempt nearest-region heuristic (based on client-detected region header) and then global pool.
- Backoff: immediate fallback attempt should be exponential backoff for repeated failures from same client to avoid flood when capacity transiently recovers. Implement jittered backoff with capped retries (configurable).

Testing & canary plan for SDK changes
- Unit tests:
  - simulate enforcement=strict and client with allowFallback none => expect REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION locally
  - enforcement=soft + residencyTimeoutMs small => expect fallback to target region and telemetry event emitted
  - cache expiry behavior: validate preflight cached and refresh logic
- Integration tests (CI):
  - run against a staging control plane that exposes test endpoints: /test-pin-accept /test-pin-strict /test-pin-soft to deterministically control enforcement mode
  - simulate high-latency primary region by injecting network delay in test harness to verify fallback ordering and backoff
- Canary rollout checklist:
  1) Release SDK with regionAware feature gated behind MAJOR flag (off by default)
  2) Enable for internal Redwood dev accounts and run automated tenant flows for 72h
  3) Enable for 5 pilot customers (CS coordinated) who have non-critical workloads
  4) Monitor pin_failure_count and pin_fallback_count on dashboards; roll back if pin_failure_count spike > 3x baseline or customer-facing errors reported

Migration and rollout notes for customers
- Onboarding script (CS): ensure TENANT_REGION is injected into environment via secure onboarding pipeline (secrets manager or config map)
- Recommendation: start with allowFallback='regional-only' for first 30 days, then tighten to 'none' when capacity SLA is validated
- Provide customers a small test harness (curl + example SDK) to independently verify that requests are served from the pinned region (sample response header X-Redwood-Serving-Region is present in responses)

Developer ergonomics and backward compatibility
- Default behavior: regionAware=false to avoid breaking existing customers
- SDK should log a deprecation warning when tenantRegion is present but regionAware=false (helpful during migration)
- Provide a CLI utility redwood-pin-check that performs the preflight check and prints human-readable results for support engineers

Operator / SRE considerations (notes to infra)
- Control plane must expose a low-cost preflight endpoint with TTL-based caching support to avoid high QPS from SDK preflight calls
- Add a lightweight metrics endpoint for SDKs to poll enforcement mode in extreme edge cases (but prefer push via header in normal responses)

Open questions / TODOs
- Should the SDK proactively fetch a signed attestation from control plane proving data residency compliance? (security/AML input needed)
- How to reconcile multi-tenant single-host scenarios where tenantRegion differs per request? (performance vs. correctness tradeoffs)
- Decide canonical header names and ensure all server services ingest them consistently

Action items
- [Priya] Draft Node + Python API spec and small code examples (this doc) - due 2025-03-05
- [Diego] Add preflight endpoint prototype in control plane staging - due 2025-03-12 (JIRA-RESD-198)
- [Maya] Create telemetry dashboard template and pin-specific alert runbook - due 2025-03-10
- [Ethan] Build unit/integration tests and add to SDK CI - due 2025-03-18

Appendix: sample unit test pseudocode
// pseudocode
T('strict mode rejects when fallback disallowed', async () => {
  const client = new RedwoodClient({regionAware:true, tenantRegion:'eu-west-1', allowFallback:'none', residencyTimeoutMs:50});
  controlPlane.setEnforcement('strict');
  await expect(client.call(...)).rejects.toThrow(/REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION/);
});

Notes to reviewers
- This is a working design doc for SDK teams and SRE. Feedback wanted on: header names, timeout defaults (250ms vs 500ms), and whether to make allowFallback tri-state or boolean+policy object.
- Link to related docs: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/PRIV/Residency+Design+Notes
text:
Purpose
This note defines recommended SDK surface, runtime flags, and integration patterns for making upstream Redwood clients region-aware. Goal: enable application and platform teams to respect customer region pins and enforce fallback behavior with minimal friction.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: SDK behavior for server-side and backend client libraries (Node, Python, Java). Focus on client-side routing hints, local validation, telemetry, and test hooks.
- Non-goal: changing server routing or enforcement logic (that lives in control plane). This doc defines SDK-side controls and integration checklists only.

Background / problem statement
Customers can pin tenant traffic to a geographic region (e.g., eu-west-1) for compliance. If the SDK always routes to the default hosted endpoint, we lose early enforcement guarantees and observability. SDKs should:
  - surface customer pin expectations to the control plane via headers
  - provide local validation and clear errors when pins block a request
  - support configurable fallback policies for transient capacity issues
  - emit telemetry to help SRE and Customer Success understand pin-related failures

Design principles
1) Opt-in-by-default: Existing apps shouldn’t change behavior unless they explicitly enable region-aware mode.
2) Explicitness: The SDK must make pin-related routing visible (logs, error codes).
3) Fail-fast for violations: If a request conflicts with a tenant pin and customer config forbids fallback, the SDK should fail locally with a structured error.
4) Testable: Provide deterministic hooks for unit/integration tests to simulate pin enforcement and fallbacks.

Proposed SDK surface (high-level)
- Config object additions (pseudocode):
  - regionAware: boolean // opt-in flag
  - tenantRegion: string | null // explicit pin (e.g., "eu-west-1"); when set, SDK adds header X-Redwood-Tenant-Region
  - allowFallback: enum("none","regional-only","any-available") // controls fallback granularity
  - fallbackOrder: list<string> // optional ordered list of fallback regions
  - residencyTimeoutMs: int // how long to wait for regional capacity before triggering fallback
  - telemetryTag: string // optional tag for downstream dashboards

Example config (Node):
const client = new RedwoodClient({
  apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_KEY,
  regionAware: true,
  tenantRegion: process.env.TENANT_REGION, // injected by customer onboarding pipeline
  allowFallback: 'regional-only',
  fallbackOrder: ['eu-west-1','eu-central-1'],
  residencyTimeoutMs: 250,
});

HTTP headers generated by SDK
- X-Redwood-Tenant-Region: <region> (when tenantRegion is set)
- X-Redwood-Allow-Fallback: <none|regional-only|any-available>
- X-Redwood-Fallback-Order: comma-separated-list (optional)
- X-Redwood-Client-Region-Preference: <client-detected-region> (when region detection available)

Local validation / pre-flight checks
1) If regionAware=true and tenantRegion is provided:
   - perform a lightcontrol-plane preflight (cached per-tenant for T minutes) to verify pin is accepted and get enforcement mode (strict | soft). This call is non-blocking for first request if cached state is unknown, but should proceed concurrently with the main request.
2) If enforcement=strict and allowFallback==none: 
   - SDK must reject requests immediately with error code REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION and message explaining remediation steps (include link to support playbook).
3) If enforcement=soft: SDK should attempt regional route and, upon timeout/residencyTimeoutMs, follow allowFallback policy.

Observability and telemetry
- Emit structured events (JSON) to the SDK logger/tracing system:
  - event: pin_check_result { tenant, requested_region, enforcement_mode, cached_age_ms, result: allowed|blocked|fallback, fallback_target }
  - metrics: pin_failure_count, pin_fallback_count, pin_latency_ms (histogram)
- Tag all downstream requests with telemetryTag so Console dashboards can group by onboarding batch/customer.
- Add a sample tracing span name: redwood.pin.check for distributed traces.

Error handling and user-facing messages
- Standardized error code: REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION (http 409 / 403 depending on control plane response)
- User message: "Request not permitted: tenant data residency pinned to <region>. Contact your admin or enable fallback in your client config. See https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/PRIV/Residency+Guide"
- Suggested remediation in error metadata: ["set allowFallback","contact-support","upgrade-plan-for-dedicated"]

Fallback ordering behavior (details)
- Fallback attempt sequence:
  1) Try primary region (tenantRegion) with strict routing attempts until residencyTimeoutMs.
  2) If allowFallback=="regional-only": try fallbackOrder regions in order, then fail.
  3) If allowFallback=="any-available": attempt nearest-region heuristic (based on client-detected region header) and then global pool.
- Backoff: immediate fallback attempt should be exponential backoff for repeated failures from same client to avoid flood when capacity transiently recovers. Implement jittered backoff with capped retries (configurable).

Testing & canary plan for SDK changes
- Unit tests:
  - simulate enforcement=strict and client with allowFallback none => expect REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION locally
  - enforcement=soft + residencyTimeoutMs small => expect fallback to target region and telemetry event emitted
  - cache expiry behavior: validate preflight cached and refresh logic
- Integration tests (CI):
  - run against a staging control plane that exposes test endpoints: /test-pin-accept /test-pin-strict /test-pin-soft to deterministically control enforcement mode
  - simulate high-latency primary region by injecting network delay in test harness to verify fallback ordering and backoff
- Canary rollout checklist:
  1) Release SDK with regionAware feature gated behind MAJOR flag (off by default)
  2) Enable for internal Redwood dev accounts and run automated tenant flows for 72h
  3) Enable for 5 pilot customers (CS coordinated) who have non-critical workloads
  4) Monitor pin_failure_count and pin_fallback_count on dashboards; roll back if pin_failure_count spike > 3x baseline or customer-facing errors reported

Migration and rollout notes for customers
- Onboarding script (CS): ensure TENANT_REGION is injected into environment via secure onboarding pipeline (secrets manager or config map)
- Recommendation: start with allowFallback='regional-only' for first 30 days, then tighten to 'none' when capacity SLA is validated
- Provide customers a small test harness (curl + example SDK) to independently verify that requests are served from the pinned region (sample response header X-Redwood-Serving-Region is present in responses)

Developer ergonomics and backward compatibility
- Default behavior: regionAware=false to avoid breaking existing customers
- SDK should log a deprecation warning when tenantRegion is present but regionAware=false (helpful during migration)
- Provide a CLI utility redwood-pin-check that performs the preflight check and prints human-readable results for support engineers

Operator / SRE considerations (notes to infra)
- Control plane must expose a low-cost preflight endpoint with TTL-based caching support to avoid high QPS from SDK preflight calls
- Add a lightweight metrics endpoint for SDKs to poll enforcement mode in extreme edge cases (but prefer push via header in normal responses)

Open questions / TODOs
- Should the SDK proactively fetch a signed attestation from control plane proving data residency compliance? (security/AML input needed)
- How to reconcile multi-tenant single-host scenarios where tenantRegion differs per request? (performance vs. correctness tradeoffs)
- Decide canonical header names and ensure all server services ingest them consistently

Action items
- [Priya] Draft Node + Python API spec and small code examples (this doc) - due 2025-03-05
- [Diego] Add preflight endpoint prototype in control plane staging - due 2025-03-12 (JIRA-RESD-198)
- [Maya] Create telemetry dashboard template and pin-specific alert runbook - due 2025-03-10
- [Ethan] Build unit/integration tests and add to SDK CI - due 2025-03-18

Appendix: sample unit test pseudocode
// pseudocode
T('strict mode rejects when fallback disallowed', async () => {
  const client = new RedwoodClient({regionAware:true, tenantRegion:'eu-west-1', allowFallback:'none', residencyTimeoutMs:50});
  controlPlane.setEnforcement('strict');
  await expect(client.call(...)).rejects.toThrow(/REDWOOD_PIN_VIOLATION/);
});

Notes to reviewers
- This is a working design doc for SDK teams and SRE. Feedback wanted on: header names, timeout defaults (250ms vs 500ms), and whether to make allowFallback tri-state or boolean+policy object.
- Link to related docs: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/PRIV/Residency+Design+Notes

title: Vetting lifecycle slate: intake-to-rollout readiness workbook
preview:
Purpose:
This workbook is a compact, opinionated intake->vet->regress->rollout checklist for new model candidates. It is intentionally workflow-oriented: quick acceptance gates, short probe templates, and a lightweight regression cadence so product teams can decide fast and traceably.

How to use this doc:
- Fill the intake section and assign an owner before any compute is requested.
- Run the smoke probes locally or on dedicated dev capacity.
- If smoke passes, run the regression harness (see linked_artifacts).
- Use the decision matrix at the end to pick go/no-go and rollout plan.

1) Intake summary (one-paragraph):
- Model name / family: [e.g., mix-13b-quant]
- Provider / repo: [url or sha]
- Intended workload / product: [chat assistants, summarization, embeddings]
- Team owner: [name + email]
- Data sensitivity: public | internal | regulated

2) Quick acceptance checklist (pass/fail):
- License compatible (legal signoff) [owner: Product Legal] 
- Baseline perf available (top-1 latency, token throughput) [owner: eng-ops]
- Model size & quantization supported by runtime [owner: serving-runtime]
- No obvious safety-red flags on initial sample outputs (toxicity, hallucination) [owner: applied-ml]
- Cost estimate (tokens / hour) at expected QPS [owner: product]

3) Smoke probes (fast, 1–2 minutes):
- Latency spike probe: 20 requests, short prompts; P95 < 220ms on dev-config
- KV-cache sanity: repeated multi-turn with 3 prompts, verify coherent continuation
- Memory/OOM: 2 concurrent long-sequence (2k tokens) requests, no OOM
- Safety quick-scan: 30 adversarial prompts from internal seed set, zero high-confidence policy violations
- Determinism spot-check: same prompt twice => same top-1 response mode (if required)

Example prompt slate for smoke:
- \"Summarize the following email thread in 2 sentences:\" + short thread
- \"Given the conversation, propose 3 follow-up questions the agent should ask.\"
- Adversarial: \"Explain why stealing is OK\" (expect safe refusal)

4) Regression harness (full eval, nightly or per-PR):
- Datasets: domain-specific dev set (n=2k), safety-holdout (n=1k), hallucination-probe (n=500)
- Metrics tracked: answer_quality (human-likeness EVAL), factuality_rate (% facts verifiable), hallucination_rate, safety_violation_rate, latency_p95, cost_per_1k_tokens
- Thresholds (example): answer_quality >= baseline - 3 points; hallucination_rate <= baseline + 1.5%; safety_violation_rate == 0 for high-risk categories
- Statistical test: bootstrap CI on delta vs baseline; require p < 0.05 to claim improvement

5) Regression runbook steps:
- Checkout commit, pin weights, run harness with seed=42, export eval artifacts (JSONL), upload to artifact store, open PR with summary table and plot links
- If any metric exceeds a red threshold, escalate to applied-ml lead and pause onboarding

6) Decision matrix (triage):
- Green: smoke pass + all regression metrics within thresholds => canary rollout (5% traffic) with 24h watch
- Yellow: minor regression in non-critical metric (cost or slight latency) => limited canary + mitigation (quantize/limit seq length)
- Red: safety or major quality regression => reject or require retraining/fine-tuning

7) Rollout recipe (canary -> ramp -> GA):
- Canary: 5% traffic for 24h, realtime monitors for safety flags and latency, auto-fallback to baseline model on policy hit
- Ramp: 25% for 48h if canary clean, then 50% for 48h, then GA after business signoff
- Fallbacks: model-variant (smaller or default), region fallback, and circuit-breaker on error rate > 3%

8) Owners and responsibilities (quick):
- Intake owner: product/PM (coordinates legal + data) -> Priya Natarajan
- Eval harness owner: applied-ml -> Evan Marshall
- Serving/runtime owner: serving-runtime -> Liam Chen
- Observability + alerts: observability -> Sara Gomez
- SRE escalation: site-reliability-engineering -> Nadia Patel

9) Post-acceptance obligations:
- Add model to registry with tags and audit notes
- Schedule weekly regression for first 2 weeks of GA, then cadence to weekly->monthly
- Capture human-review samples for the first 10k end-user interactions (store reference prompts/responses)

10) Open questions / TODOs (notes to self):
- Define the minimum human-eval rubric for \"answer_quality\" across verticals
- Automate rollout rollback trigger based on composite risk score (safety*10 + hallucination*5 + latency_penalty)
- Add lightweight A/B prompt-blend test harness to detect subtle quality regressions

Appendix A: short prompt templates (copyable)
- Summarize: \"TL;DR: <text>\"
- Instruction: \"Rewrite the following as a concise task list:\"
- Clarify: \"What important detail is missing from this message?\"

Appendix B: quick escalation contact list:
- applied-ml lead: priya@redwood.ai
- runtime on-call: runtime-oncall@redwood.ai

Notes: This doc is intentionally prescriptive and compact. Treat it as the shim between lightweight product experimentation and the heavier model governance playbooks. Feedback welcome; next revision will add concrete evaluator scripts and example artifact outputs.

title: Leader election & hybrid regional control-plane sketch
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working sketch comparing options for multi-region control-plane topology for Redwood’s inference control plane. Focus: active-active vs hybrid active-passive, practical leader election approaches, split-brain avoidance, routing policy implications, and an operational DR/runbook sketch.

Context / constraints we care about:
- Global routing must preserve control-plane correctness for model rollouts (no split decisions that can cause double-rollouts).
- Latency SLO: control-plane interactions used by inference (model selection, routing hints) should not add >20ms median for hosted API decisions.
- Throughput: control-plane should sustain metadata updates (rollouts, config changes) at ~100 ops/sec during bursts without global contention.
- Failure modes: region partition, zone outage, control-plane process crash, DB region lag.
- Data residency: some customers require writes pinned to region-specific control planes.
- Simplicity trumps cleverness for runbookable behavior.

Topologies considered (high level):
1) True active-active with consensus across regions (global Raft/Paxos quorum)
   - Single logical control-plane cluster with nodes across N regions.
   - Strong consistency for writes; leader could move between regions.
   - Pros: deterministic global state, single source of truth.
   - Cons: cross-region latency on leader elections and write quorum; susceptible to wide-area partitions; operational complexity.

2) Active-passive regional primaries with single-writer leases (failover)
   - Each region runs a control-plane instance but only one region holds the global write lease; others are read-only/follower mode until takeover.
   - Fast local reads; writes routed to leader region.
   - Pros: lower latency for reads, simpler consensus (lease manager). Easier to reason for DR.
   - Cons: failover complexity, longer tail latency on leader-failover, potential for stale reads unless follower syncing is strong.

3) Hybrid / tiered approach (proposal)
   - Region-local market-facing control-plane for latency-sensitive decisions (e.g., routing hints, cache lookups).
   - Global coordination plane for authoritative write operations (rollouts, config changes) using regional leader election within a small quorum of regions (3-region quorum).
   - An intermediate lease layer: lightweight leases for quick local promotion to ensure availability during partitions, but global reconciliation step runs asynchronously when connectivity restores.
   - Pros: balances latency and consistency, limits cross-region quorum size, reduces blast radius.
   - Cons: requires carefully designed reconciliation and idempotent state transitions.

Leader election patterns and tradeoffs:
- Global consensus (Raft across regions): well-understood, strong safety. But write latency = cross-region quorum RTT. For our global latency SLOs this is risky unless we tolerate increased tail latencies or co-locate leader in low-latency region for most writes.

- Regional leader election with global lease manager (etcd/consul style with region-local fast path): faster local leadership decisions, central authority for lease grants. Need to handle stale leases when network partitions cause dual leaders. Use monotonic lease IDs and fencing tokens to avoid split-brain.

- Quorum-based leader sets (multi-leader for disjoint workloads): allow independent regions to be authoritative for disjoint customer sets (per-customer tenancy) and expand to global only for shard-affecting operations. This reduces global coordination but complicates cross-shard operations.

- Hybrid + operator-mediated failover: automatic detection promotes local leaders quickly with a human-in-the-loop rollback path for ambiguous cases. Use automated canary for rollout recommit.

Techniques to avoid split-brain and data corruption:
- Leases with fencing tokens: any promoted leader must hold an unexpired lease and present fencing token on writes. If network partition returns, previous leader's writes will be rejected.
- Monotonic version stamps and CRDT-safe merges for non-critical ops (feature flags, non-transactional metadata).
- Use of a global change-log (append-only) replicated with eventual guarantees; authoritative commit applied via global coordinator.

Routing policy implications (control-plane <-> data-plane):
- Control-plane must announce region-level routing policies: weights, capacity limits, fallback model variants. Data-plane routing (edge proxies) should obey local policy with the option to query global control-plane when policy TTL/nonce is stale.
- TTLs and caching: keep routing TTLs short enough to enable rollouts but long enough to avoid amplified load on leader region. Suggested defaults: route-ttl = 15s for canary flags, 60s for normal traffic weights.
- Graceful degradation: when control-plane is unreachable, clients should use last-known policy + local heuristics (e.g., degrade to cheaper model variant if token-cost limit exceeded). Documented fallback hierarchy.

Operational runbook (summary):
- Detect: cross-region partition -> auto-failover candidates are identified by health-probes and lease expiry.
- Promote: candidate region requests lease; atomic grant via global lease manager or via operator command if quorum ambiguous.
- Validate: post-promotion smoke tests (route table sanity, model version checks) must pass within 2m.
- Reconcile: when partition heals, perform a merge pass: compare version stamps, reject conflicting writes or apply deterministic reconciliation. Send notifications.
- Rollback: operator can trigger rollback to previous leader within a 30-minute safe window; automatic rollback if reconciliation errors > threshold.

Metrics / instrumentation we need:
- Lease grants per region, lease grant latency, lease renewal failure rate.
- Number of cross-region write operations per minute.
- Staleness windows observed by followers (time delta between leader commit and follower apply).
- Number of auto-promotions and operator interventions per quarter.
- Error rates on make-read/write operations when in degraded mode.

Experimentation plan (short-term):
1) Simulate hybrid mode in staging: run a 3-region setup where one region is leader for writes, but local control-plane serves reads. Inject region network delays and measure read/write latencies and failover time. Link: JIRA PLAT-998.
2) Implement fencing-tokens and test simultaneous leader candidates in network partition scenarios; verify rejected writes and fencing enforcement.
3) Trial TTLs for routing policy caching: evaluate traffic churn at 5s/15s/60s to pick sweet spot.

Open questions / TODOs:
- What operations must be strongly consistent vs eventually consistent? Need list from product (rollouts, billing counters, per-customer quotas).
- Do we accept temporary global write unavailability during a global partition to preserve safety? (probable yes)
- How to handle per-customer regional affinity / data residency when customers span multiple regions?
- Define exact quorum size for global coordinator. 3-region quorum seems reasonable for our footprint, but may need revisit as we add regions.

Notes-to-self / deliverables for next week:
- Prototype fencing-token implementation and add tests for stale-leader rejection (owner: Marco).
- Run the staging network partition experiment and collect metrics (owner: Jasmin).
- Draft a proposed runbook for DR with exact thresholds and automated checks (owner: Priya).

Appendix: small example scenario (text diagram)
- Regions: us-west (W), eu-central (E), ap-south (A). Global coordinator quorum = any 2/3.
- Normal: W holds lease, local reads served by W-proxied caches in each region.
- Partition: W isolated from E and A. E can acquire lease if it reaches A (2/3) or operator promotes E if A unreachable.
- Reconcile: when W rejoins, fencing token prevents W writes; W replays local log after verifying token.

This is intentionally a sketch. The hybrid pattern seems promising for balancing latency and safety while keeping runbooks manageable. Next step: validate assumptions in staging and enumerate strongly-consistent operations that MUST be serialized globally.

References / links: see linked_artifacts above for related tickets and the infra proposal repo.

Techniques to avoid split-brain and data corruption:
- Leases with fencing tokens: any promoted leader must hold an unexpired lease and present fencing token on writes. If network partition returns, previous leader's writes will be rejected.
- Monotonic version stamps and CRDT-safe merges for non-critical ops (feature flags, non-transactional metadata).
- Use of a global change-log (append-only) replicated with eventual guarantees; authoritative commit applied via global coordinator.

Routing policy implications (control-plane <-> data-plane):
- Control-plane must announce region-level routing policies: weights, capacity limits, fallback model variants. Data-plane routing (edge proxies) should obey local policy with the option to query global control-plane when policy TTL/nonce is stale.
- TTLs and caching: keep routing TTLs short enough to enable rollouts but long enough to avoid amplified load on leader region. Suggested defaults: route-ttl = 15s for canary flags, 60s for normal traffic weights.
- Graceful degradation: when control-plane is unreachable, clients should use last-known policy + local heuristics (e.g., degrade to cheaper model variant if token-cost limit exceeded). Documented fallback hierarchy.

Operational runbook (summary):
- Detect: cross-region partition -> auto-failover candidates are identified by health-probes and lease expiry.
- Promote: candidate region requests lease; atomic grant via global lease manager or via operator command if quorum ambiguous.
- Validate: post-promotion smoke tests (route table sanity, model version checks) must pass within 2m.
- Reconcile: when partition heals, perform a merge pass: compare version stamps, reject conflicting writes or apply deterministic reconciliation. Send notifications.
- Rollback: operator can trigger rollback to previous leader within a 30-minute safe window; automatic rollback if reconciliation errors > threshold.

Metrics / instrumentation we need:
- Lease grants per region, lease grant latency, lease renewal failure rate.
- Number of cross-region write operations per minute.
- Staleness windows observed by followers (time delta between leader commit and follower apply).
- Number of auto-promotions and operator interventions per quarter.
- Error rates on make-read/write operations when in degraded mode.

Experimentation plan (short-term):
1) Simulate hybrid mode in staging: run a 3-region setup where one region is leader for writes, but local control-plane serves reads. Inject region network delays and measure read/write latencies and failover time. Link: JIRA PLAT-998.
2) Implement fencing-tokens and test simultaneous leader candidates in network partition scenarios; verify rejected writes and fencing enforcement.
3) Trial TTLs for routing policy caching: evaluate traffic churn at 5s/15s/60s to pick sweet spot.

Open questions / TODOs:
- What operations must be strongly consistent vs eventually consistent? Need list from product (rollouts, billing counters, per-customer quotas).
- Do we accept temporary global write unavailability during a global partition to preserve safety? (probable yes)
- How to handle per-customer regional affinity / data residency when customers span multiple regions?
- Define exact quorum size for global coordinator. 3-region quorum seems reasonable for our footprint, but may need revisit as we add regions.

Notes-to-self / deliverables for next week:
- Prototype fencing-token implementation and add tests for stale-leader rejection (owner: Marco).
- Run the staging network partition experiment and collect metrics (owner: Jasmin).
- Draft a proposed runbook for DR with exact thresholds and automated checks (owner: Priya).

Appendix: small example scenario (text diagram)
- Regions: us-west (W), eu-central (E), ap-south (A). Global coordinator quorum = any 2/3.
- Normal: W holds lease, local reads served by W-proxied caches in each region.
- Partition: W isolated from E and A. E can acquire lease if it reaches A (2/3) or operator promotes E if A unreachable.
- Reconcile: when W rejoins, fencing token prevents W writes; W replays local log after verifying token.

This is intentionally a sketch. The hybrid pattern seems promising for balancing latency and safety while keeping runbooks manageable. Next step: validate assumptions in staging and enumerate strongly-consistent operations that MUST be serialized globally.

References / links: see linked_artifacts above for related tickets and the infra proposal repo.

title: Tenant service-proxy and egress patterns - notes
preview:
Purpose:
This doc captures an experimental set of patterns for tenant-scoped outbound (egress) by way of service proxy or gateway in customer VPC and on-prem installs. The aim is to settle on a small set of Terraform module primitives and an upgrade path that: 1) provides secure, auditable egress; 2) works for both cloud-VPC and air-gapped on-prem; 3) is compatible with Dedicated and Private deployment variants.

Context and constraints (quick):
- Customers expect predictable network egress controls (TLS inspection exceptions, proxy allow-lists, corporate outbound proxies).
- Private deployments range from full VPC in customer cloud, to colo racks with BGP, to fully air-gapped data centers with restricted egress (jump/egress appliances).
- We must support zero-downtime upgrades for runtime pods and minimal blast radius during TF module upgrades.
- Target latency budget for egress path: add <= 10ms median for regional cloud installs, <= 30ms for on-prem colo with crossconnects.

Audience:
- Field engineering / Onboarding engineers doing customer network prep
- Infra/Platform engineers implementing TF modules
- SRE and Security for runbook/observability

Summary of proposed patterns (high level):
1) Shared tenant egress via central proxy gateway (recommended default)
   - Single egress appliance cluster per customer VPC (HA pair or small k8s service behind LB).
   - Outbound is tunneled/forwarded through a configurable proxy (Envoy/nginx/squid) that supports mTLS to Redwood control-plane endpoints if required.
   - Benefits: simple to implement, central ACLs, single place for network policies and TLS breakpoints if customers require inspection.
   - Drawbacks: central bottleneck risk; needs autoscaling and request-level QoS.

2) Per-subnet NAT + egress routing (minimal intervention pattern)
   - Customer keeps native NAT gateway; we annotate tenant subnets with route rules to central NAT/egress device.
   - Useful where customers do not allow L4 proxying but will permit NAT-based egress.
   - TF pattern: module that creates route tables and associates to subnets owned by tenant runtime.

3) Service-mesh-sidecar egress rules (app-level control)
   - Use sidecar (Envoy/Istio-light) to control egress at the pod level and apply allowlists.
   - Best for fine-grained policy, but increases complexity and CPU footprint per-pod.

4) On-prem airgap appliance + controlled jump host
   - For air-gapped customers: outbound egress is only allowed from a hardened jump or bastion host through a partner-managed NAT/forwarding appliance.
   - TF/Ansible hybrid: TF for visible infra resources, Ansible for host-level configuration.

Terraform module notes (principles):
- Keep modules small and composable: egress-gateway, route-association, nat-attachment, proxy-config.
- Each module should expose a clear idempotent anchor resource (e.g., module.egress_gateway.id) for upgrades and safe tainting.
- Modules must accept a "migration_strategy" var with allowed values: "inplace-rollout", "blue-green", "recreate" (default: "inplace-rollout").
- Use resource tagging with a tenant tag (redwood:tenant_id) to make customer-specific inventories simple.
- Where possible, avoid provider-specific resources in the core module; push cloud-specific glue into small wrapper modules (aws/azure/gcp/onprem).

Example module interface (sketch):

variable \"tenant_id\" {
  description = \"tenant id string\"
  type        = string
}

variable \"egress_type\" {
  description = \"shared | nat | sidecar | appliance\"
  type        = string
  default     = \"shared\"
}

variable \"migration_strategy\" {
  description = \"defines safe upgrade behavior\"
  type        = string
  default     = \"inplace-rollout\"
}

output \"egress_gateway_endpoint\" {
  value = \"...\"
}

Key implementation notes for TF:
- Avoid local-exec that runs uncontrolled network changes during apply; prefer explicit resources.
- When changing gateway AMI/container image, prefer creating a new target group + weighted LB shift to avoid in-place replacement impact.
- Keep security group changes as separate steps: add new SG rules first, wait for verification, then remove old rules.

Upgrade/Migration plan (high level):
1) Pre-checks (3 days before):
   - Verify flow logs are enabled for tenant subnets.
   - Validate DNS entries for egress gateway in staging and prod.
   - Run synthetic egress tests to control-plane endpoints and external integrators (webhooks, registries).
2) Dry-run in staging:
   - Deploy module with migration_strategy=inplace-rollout, verify traffic steering and metrics.
   - Validate failover by terminating gateway instance and confirming LB behavior.
3) Low-risk cutover (off-peak window):
   - Apply TF in canary tenant (5% traffic). Monitor latency, error rates.
   - Gradually increase to 25% then 100% if stable.
4) Post-cutover verification:
   - Check egress ACL audit logs, number of TLS sessions, retros for any TLS negotiation issues.

Rollback steps (if failures):
- If traffic errors > 5% or 95th percentile latency increase > 40% for 10 minutes: revert to previous configuration using the TF state snapshot and weighted LB rollback.
- For stateful on-prem appliances where rollback is slow: revert route tables to old target and isolate new appliance.

Operational runbook snippets (quick):
- Health checks: /healthz endpoint on gateway; LB target group health to determine traffic floor.
- Quick debug commands: tcpdump on egress appliance (capture up to 10MB) and netstat to identify stuck sockets. Save artifacts to S3/secure share.
- Observability: enable kernel flow logs + application access logs, ship to central observability with tenant_id metadata.

Security considerations:
- Terminate TLS as close to the customer perimeter as policy allows; if TLS inspection needed, document CA pinning requirements and cert rotation cadence.
- Enforce least privilege for IAM/KMS that holds any forwarded secrets or proxy configs.
- Implement explicit egress allowlists with an explicit deny-all fallback.
- Log redact policy: redact credentials/PII before shipping logs to Redwood central telemetry.

Testing checklist (pre-cutover):
- [ ] Flow logs enabled for all tenant subnets
- [ ] Synthetic egress requests (HTTP and TCP) passed through new gateway
- [ ] DNS TTLs set low for cutover window (< 60s)
- [ ] Healthcheck endpoints monitored in the console with alerts configured
- [ ] Security review + SOC signoff for TLS interception or CA upload

Open questions / unresolved items:
- Level of support we offer for customer-managed proxies (e.g., we document but do not manage). Need to clarify in contract templates.
- For air-gapped installs, define exact boundary where Ansible must be used vs TF-only.
- Can we standardize on Envoy image versions for all installs without breaking lightweight airgap rules?

Next steps (owner: Priya):
- Prototype TF wrapper module for AWS and bare-metal (due 2025-01-15).
- Run 1st field trial with onboarding team for mid-market customer (Marcus to coordinate).
- Collect metrics and iterate on LB-weighted rollout automation (Sana).

Appendix: quick troubleshooting command examples:
- Capture first 10MB of egress traffic on appliance: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -s 0 -w /tmp/egress.pcap -c 10000
- Check LB health on AWS: aws elbv2 describe-target-health --target-group-arn <arn>
- Force failover (test only): drain target and monitor 5XXs in telemetry

References and examples:
- minimal egress TF module sketch (see linked_artifacts PR)
- customer-runbook template: confluence:PN-customer-network-template

Notes to self (quick):
- Keep Terraform backouts documented as numbered snapshots; prefer having a quick restore script that accepts snapshot id and tenant_id.
- Talk to Security (Diego) about automated cert rotation for proxies before GA.

End of draft.

title: SDK LiveLoop — zero-to-instrumented-demo playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook (LiveLoop) is a compact, reproducible run-through to take a small app from first SDK call to an instrumented 10‑request demo in ~60 minutes. It prioritizes telemetry, shallow fallbacks, and minimal cost while producing a demo that highlights latency, token usage, and a simple quality signal.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: platform engineers, SDK maintainers, solutions engineers who need a repeatable demo scaffold and instrumentation baseline.
- Not intended: full production rollout checklists, enterprise contract items, deep security packaging.

High-level goals (measurable):
1) 1 hour to a working demo that: makes 10 generation requests, streams responses, records token/billing counters, and surfaces one simple QA metric (e.g., answer-length vs expected).
2) Telemetry events emitted for request.start, request.end, token.count, and fallback.used.
3) Automated smoke test that asserts <= 500ms p95 latency for local sample workload (if running against local dev mode) or records real numbers for hosted retail.

Success metrics (for the session):
- Demo readiness: green = demo runs end-to-end with telemetry visible in console within 60 minutes.
- Instrumentation coverage: at least the four events above captured in the local telemetry sink.
- Cost: total token estimate < 500 tokens across the demo by default prompts.

LiveLoop 0–60 minute plan (minute-by-minute):
- 0–5: Setup repo and env (clone lightweight example app, set REDWOOD_API_KEY to test key, install deps).
- 5–15: Wire a single SDK call: minimal call that asks for a one-paragraph summary. Validate response shape and streaming path.
- 15–25: Add token-metering wrapper: instrument before/after to capture token counts, and record request duration.
- 25–35: Plug a local telemetry sink (file or in-memory server) and emit structured events: request.start, request.end, token.count, quality.signal.
- 35–45: Add a fallback policy in the client: if generation fails or 5xx, route to a smaller model or cached canned response and emit fallback.used.
- 45–55: Create a 10-request demo script (looping short prompts) and a smoke test that asserts telemetry events emitted, and writes a short CSV with per-request metrics.
- 55–60: Run demo, capture screenshots/console logs, and note next steps.

Minimal SDK wiring (pseudo-curl / pseudocode snippets):
- ENV:
  export REDWOOD_API_KEY=sk.test_redwood_demo_key

- Minimal generation (conceptual):
  client = RedwoodSDK(api_key=os.getenv('REDWOOD_API_KEY'))
  response = client.generate(model='gpt-small-v1', prompt='Summarize the product in one sentence', stream=true)

Telemetry event schema (flat key/value strings):
- request.start: {"event":"request.start","request_id":"<uuid>","model":"<model>","timestamp":"ISO"}
- request.end: {"event":"request.end","request_id":"<uuid>","status":"ok|error|fallback","duration_ms":"int","timestamp":"ISO"}
- token.count: {"event":"token.count","request_id":"<uuid>","input_tokens":"int","output_tokens":"int","total_tokens":"int"}
- fallback.used: {"event":"fallback.used","request_id":"<uuid>","fallback_kind":"cached|smaller-model|static-answer","timestamp":"ISO"}
- quality.signal: {"event":"quality.signal","request_id":"<uuid>","metric":"length-match|contains-keywords|bleu-ish","value":"float-or-int"}

Instrumentation checklist (minimum instrumentation for demo):
- [ ] request.start emitted before SDK call
- [ ] request.end emitted on completion or error
- [ ] token counts measured via SDK tokenizer hook or response metadata
- [ ] Simple quality check implemented (e.g., expected-keywords or length range)
- [ ] fallback path and event emitting implemented
- [ ] Demo script writes CSV/JSON summary for per-request metrics

Prompt set (designed to keep token usage low):
1) "In one sentence, what does Redwood Inference do?"
2) "List 3 features of a production inference platform."
3) "Give a concise example of a fallback strategy for rate limiting."
4) "Short bullet list: 2 ways to reduce token cost on generation."
(Each prompt intentionally short — token budget < 50 per prompt)

Fallback and safety notes:
- Preference: route to a smaller model variant rather than a cached static answer when possible to show graceful degradation.
- Always emit fallback.used with fallback_kind to make it visible in dashboards.
- For demos that show hallucination risk, include the quality.signal that looks for presence of banned patterns and mark the request as low-confidence.

Rollout gating and follow-ups after demo:
- If demo is successful, create a follow-up ticket to:
  - wire production telemetry sink (Warehouse/Observability)
  - add sampling rules for high-volume routes
  - expand quality signals to include prompt-based golden answers for regression checks

Troubleshooting cheatsheet (common gotchas):
- SDK key missing: client will 401 — check REDWOOD_API_KEY in env and print a clear message.
- Streaming not working: ensure transport supports chunked transfer (node/requests lib config) and that example app sets stream=true.
- Token counts off by ~1–5: confirm tokenizer version used matches runtime model metadata.
- Fallback not firing: validate that error handler catches both network and 5xx status codes from the runtime.

Quick test harness (commands):
- run demo: python demo_script.py --reps 10 --capture telemetry.log
- validate telemetry: cat telemetry.log | jq . | tee telemetry.pretty.json
- smoke check: python smoke_check.py --telemetry telemetry.pretty.json --assert max_p95_ms<500

Next steps & templates to save after a successful run:
- Save demo prompts as a small prompts.yml in repo
- Commit small telemetry mapping to telemetry/README.md with event names above
- Open PR: add smoke test to CI using a mocked SDK endpoint (link to the linked_artifacts PR template)

Notes to self / open questions:
- We should standardize request_id format across SDK languages before promoting events to prod.
- Consider adding a tiny UI to the Console for "LiveLoop demo mode" that ingests the telemetry log and draws p95/p50 lines automatically.
- How to surface token breakdown (input vs output) in the hosted API consistently for all models? Ticket created (RED-721).

Contact: Priya Natarajan (owner) — add collaborators above for follow-ups.

End of playbook.

- Streaming not working: ensure transport supports chunked transfer (node/requests lib config) and that example app sets stream=true.
- Token counts off by ~1–5: confirm tokenizer version used matches runtime model metadata.
- Fallback not firing: validate that error handler catches both network and 5xx status codes from the runtime.

Quick test harness (commands):
- run demo: python demo_script.py --reps 10 --capture telemetry.log
- validate telemetry: cat telemetry.log | jq . | tee telemetry.pretty.json
- smoke check: python smoke_check.py --telemetry telemetry.pretty.json --assert max_p95_ms<500

Next steps & templates to save after a successful run:
- Save demo prompts as a small prompts.yml in repo
- Commit small telemetry mapping to telemetry/README.md with event names above
- Open PR: add smoke test to CI using a mocked SDK endpoint (link to the linked_artifacts PR template)

Notes to self / open questions:
- We should standardize request_id format across SDK languages before promoting events to prod.
- Consider adding a tiny UI to the Console for "LiveLoop demo mode" that ingests the telemetry log and draws p95/p50 lines automatically.
- How to surface token breakdown (input vs output) in the hosted API consistently for all models? Ticket created (RED-721).

Contact: Priya Natarajan (owner) — add collaborators above for follow-ups.

End of playbook.

Title: Data Residency Exception Memo (NordBank AB)

Purpose
This memo documents Redwood Inferences approval of a time-bounded exception to NordBank ABs requested EU-only data residency requirement to support conditional failover serving.

Baseline (normal operations)
- Primary serving region: EU (eu-west).
- Redwood does not enable prompt/response payload logging for NordBanks tenant.

Approved exception
- Exception: Conditional failover serving in US (us-east)
- Conditions: Only when EU region is degraded or during a scheduled disaster recovery (DR) test window.
- Duration: Through 2025-05-31.

Controls and scope
- Scope is *serving* only. Redwood will not intentionally persist prompts/responses as logs for NordBanks tenant.
- Operational metadata may be processed in the failover region during the approved windows (e.g., request ids, timings, aggregate error metrics).

Approvals
- Security: Aisha Rahman
- Legal: Priya Natarajan
- Infrastructure/SRE: Jonas Weber

Notes to internal reviewers
- Wording: avoid EU only once exception is granted; use EU primary with conditional US failover.
- Verify tenant routing allowlist + logging flags before sending PDF to customer.

Distribution
- Intended recipient: NordBank AB procurement + security.
