title: Active-passive region routing simulation and runbook
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working note + operational runbook for active/passive regional steering (primary region + hot-standby) used during capacity failures, cross-region latency spikes, and control-plane instability. The doc mixes architecture rationale, quick-play mitigations, and simulation results. Treat as a living artifact: iterate after each incident.

High-level goals:
- Keep <100ms added tail latency for 95th pct of requests when failing over.
- Avoid double-executing long-running requests during rollovers.
- Preserve model-version affinity where possible to avoid quality regressions.
- Provide deterministic, auditable decision steps for on-call.

Assumptions / constraints:
- Each customer has a primary region (P) and a configured passive region (S). Traffic normally routes to P.
- Control plane stores steering policies in CP config service with strong consistency (leader-based raft).
- Edge proxies subscribe to CP via streaming deltas; they may be up to 10s behind after partition.
- Model variants: vA (primary), vA-quant (compatible fallback).
- KV-prefix caching exists per-region; cache warming on failover may take 2–8s depending on model + token prefix distribution.

Architecture notes (brief):
- Control plane components: policy store (raft), decision API (read-only for proxies), rollout coordinator (for canaries), telemetry ingester (metrics/traces).
- Data-plane: regional proxy -> local serving pool -> model instance. Proxies honor steering header TTL and idempotency tokens.
- Heartbeats: region health derived from p99 latency, request success rate, and GPU saturation.

Telemetry to watch (dashboard names):
- CP::API-latency (avg/95/99)
- Steering::Policy-applied-rate (per-region)
- Edge::Stale-configs (seconds behind)
- Serving::Model-variant-error-rate (per-variant)
- Serving::KV-cache-hit-rate (prefix-level)
- Platform::Cross-region-network-loss (BGP/VPCE metrics)

Runbook: Incident scenarios and play actions
1) Scenario: Control plane write latency spike (raft leader overloaded)
   Detection signals: CP::API-latency > 500ms sustained for >2m AND increased Edge::Stale-configs > 30s
   Impact: policies not propagated; new route decisions fallback to last-known config -> potential capacity imbalance.
   Immediate mitigation steps:
   - PAGE CP on-call (step 0). Slack #infra-ops. Ticket JIRA-ROUTE-489.
   - Switch CP read traffic to follower read-only nodes (if allowed by config) via lb tag change: runbook-cmd: k8s patch svc/cp-decision --set \"spec.selector.version=read-follower\". Note: this is a temporary risk for stale reads.
   - If leader CPU/GPU pressure: identify heavy background jobs (snapshot/compaction). Cancel or reschedule non-critical jobs: /scripts/cp-drain-heavy-jobs.sh --dry-run then remove.
   Longer-term: increase raft quorum timeout, add small scale-out to decision API nodes, instrument CP request hedging.

2) Scenario: Region partition (P cannot reach S and control plane perceives P as degraded)
   Detection signals: Platform::Cross-region-network-loss > 2% AND Serving::Model-variant-error-rate up in P
   Impact: clients in S might be misrouted or see high latency; risk of split-brain if both regions believe they are primary.
   Mitigation steps (ordered):
   - Immediately enforce single-writer policy: confirm raft leader location. If leader in P and network partition isolates it from majority, step-down leader to avoid split-brain: runbook-cmd: kubectl exec cp-leader -- ./raft_ctl stepdown --reason \"net partition\".
   - If stepdown not possible, explicitly set read-only mode for CP via runtime flag: /api/admin?mode=read-only with header X-Operator-Auth. This prevents new write policy changes.
   - Trigger edge-level circuit: instruct proxies in isolated region to enter degraded-steer mode (local-only) to avoid sending requests cross-region. runbook-cmd: proxyctl set-mode --region P degraded-steer --ttl 300.
   - Notify SRE and on-call leads; open bridge.
   Notes: prioritize state correctness over availability for control-plane writes. For data-plane, prefer availability with conservative fallbacks (quantized models).

3) Scenario: Model variant depletion in primary (e.g., GPU OOMs -> model instances unhealthy)
   Detection: Serving::instance-restarts, GPU-mem-pressure > 90%, p95 latency spike
   Impact: throughput drop; clients may see errors or elevated tail latency.
   Fast path mitigation:
   - Activate model fallback policy: set steering policy to route a percentage to passive region or to fallback variant (vA-quant). Quick command: curl -X POST https://cp/admin/policies/fallback --body '{"route_pct":30,"variant":"vA-quant"}' -H 'X-Admin: true' (ops only).
   - Enable autoscale-burst for the affected region: k8s scale deployment/serving-pool --replicas=$(current+2) but watch for GPU capacity quotas.
   - If OOM persists, drain heavy requests (batch-size change): change runtime param for that model: /api/models/vA/config -> set max_batch_tokens=1024.

4) Scenario: Rollout (canary) goes wrong and causes quality regressions across regions
   Detection: Redwood::Quality-regression-alert (from evals) OR customer tickets spike
   Steps:
   - Immediately pause rollout: /api/admin/rollouts/{id}/pause.
   - Rollback variant in CP: /api/admin/rollouts/{id}/rollback -> confirm applied.
   - If rollback fails: set explicit steering policy pinning customers to previous model by customer-id hash mapping.
   - Post-incident: collect sample prompts, push to offline eval harness, schedule triage with ML and Product.

5) Scenario: KV cache inconsistency after failover (stale prefix responses)
   Detection: elevated Serving::inconsistent-cache-answers, customer-reported hallucination pattern tied to prefix tokens
   Impact: correctness problems on resumed traffic to S
   Mitigation:
   - Warm caches by replaying recent prefix prefixes via synthetic warmers for high-volume customers. Command: /scripts/warm-prefixes.sh --customers-file recent-top-100.json --target-region S.
   - If warming cannot catch up, temporarily pin routing for affected customers back to P until caches are rebuilt. Use policy scoping by customer id.

Escalation and communication matrix:
- Pager: Platform on-call (primary). If not acknowledged in 10m escalate to Platform lead (Avery Chen).
- Data loss / privacy risk escalate to Security (Lina Moreno) immediately.
- Exec/Customer-facing: Communicate via #incidents with one-line status updates every 15m until stable.

Playbook quick commands (cheat sheet):
- cp health: curl https://cp/healthz
- force read-only CP: curl -X POST https://cp/admin/mode -d '{"mode":"readonly"}' -H 'X-Operator-Auth:true'
- proxy set degraded: proxyctl set-mode --region <r> degraded-steer --ttl 300
- warm caches: /scripts/warm-prefixes.sh --customers-file <file> --target-region <r>
- pause rollout: /api/admin/rollouts/<id>/pause

Simulation notes (short):
- We ran a 1h synthetic test (2024-10-12) with 20% traffic diversion to S during a simulated P OOM. Result: 8s median cold-start per-model on S for first 10k requests, tail stabilized after prefix warmers. CPU of CP leader spiked during simultaneous policy updates; we throttled updates and added jitter which fixed leader CPU blowouts.

Open action items (todo):
- [ ] Add automated warmers as part of failover policy (owner: Samir).
- [ ] Add policy delta conflict detector and pre-commit hooks (owner: Priya).
- [ ] Document exact customer pinning API and bake safe templates for on-call (owner: Avery).

Postmortem checklist (if incident reaches Sev2+):
- timeline + raw metrics bucketed by 1s
- CP logs + raft debug traces
- sample requests (redacted)
- decisions matrix + rationale

Notes to self / informal thoughts:
- Consider a hybrid approach: small-active fanout to passive region for warm caches (1–5% steady sampling) to reduce cold-start surface. Need to weigh cost.
- Watch for billing blowouts when warmers run at scale (cost v. risk analysis).

References / links:
- Confluence: CONFLUENCE-PLT-231 (design doc)
- Sim harness: github.com/redwood-inference/routing-sim
- Incident: JIRA-ROUTE-489 (current active ticket)

End of doc. Keep iteration notes in-line, add simulation outputs as attachments (CSV) when available.

title: Staging-to-Prod On‑Prem Cutover Runbook + TF Quirks
preview:
Purpose:
This runbook captures pragmatic steps, validation checks, and Terraform (TF) workarounds we found while moving customer on‑prem stacks from staging to production in constrained environments. It is intended as a short, field‑friendly reference for SREs and infra engineers doing the cutover (not a policy doc).

Scope and assumptions:
- Applies to private deployments where control plane is hosted but inference nodes live in customer VPCs / on‑prem racks.
- Assumes: TF state is remote (consul/s3-like), state locking available, network peering or crossconnect already tested in staging.
- Does NOT cover application schema migrations or model weights transfer (separate playbook).

Quick summary of the approach:
1) Prepare: freeze infra changes, snapshot TF state, preflight tests in staging mirror.
2) Dry‑run: create a production namespace with traffic shadowing and smoke tests for 24–48h.
3) Canaries: route 1–5% traffic to prod nodes, run golden prompts + latency/cost probes.
4) Cutover: incremental traffic shift with gating checks and automatic rollback triggers.
5) Post‑cutover: extended validation + monitoring retention bump for 72h.

Pre‑cutover checklist (must pass before any traffic shift):
- TF state snapshot: run `terraform state pull > tfstate-backup-YYYYMMDD` and upload to customer secure storage.
- State lock health: confirm no long locks in lock table and lock TTLs are sane.
- Secrets: verify KMS/HSM key access and rotation window with customer (test encrypt/decrypt).
- Certificates: edge certs + mTLS certs validated for CN/SAN with production hostnames.
- DNS prewiring: production DNS entries created with short TTL (60s) but set to 300s during maintenance if registrar limits.
- BGP/HA failover: if using physical crossconnect, confirm BGP session establishment and route advertisements with netops.
- Observability: ensure tracing hooks and token‑level cost metrics enabled for the prod namespace.
- Backout window and stakeholders on call: list of pager contacts + escalation tree.

Terraform quirks and recommended mitigations (field notes):
- Provider version skew: some customers pin provider versions in their enterprise catalog. When bumping provider in TF modules, include a migration commit that uses `terraform init -upgrade` in a sandbox and run `terraform plan` to detect provider-induced diffs.
- Provider alias collisions across modules: when modules use provider aliases, TF may create duplicate provider configs after module ref rework. Mitigation: add explicit `provider = aws.customer` in module blocks and include a short note in the module README.
- Lifecycle ignore_changes pitfalls: we rely on `ignore_changes` for ephemeral fields (e.g., ephemeral ip). Downside — drift detection hides unintended changes. Use `ignore_changes` sparingly and document where used.
- Large TF plans time out via our CI runner for big networking changes. Workaround: break plan into smaller scopes (`terraform plan -target=module.vpc`), or run plan on a larger runner with higher timeout.
- Remote state drift after manual changes: customers sometimes perform hand edits in console. Always run `terraform refresh` as part of preflight and capture a refresh diff artifact.
- State imports: importing resources with different IDs (e.g., cloud vendor vs on‑prem controller) can require manual mapping. Keep a mapping table in the ticket and capture `terraform state mv` commands.
- Locked resources in frequent apply loops: long‑running apply scripts (build loops) can leave locks. We recommend a tagging standard and an operator script that lists locks and TTLs; avoid automated retries that bypass TTL checks.

Cutover run steps (detailed):
- T minus 72h: create production TF workspace, copy variable set but do NOT apply. Run `terraform plan` and save artifact. Share plan with customer infra lead.
- T minus 48h: spin up prod control plane agents behind a shadowed router (traffic mirrored). Run smoke suite (auth, kv cache priming, model load).
- T minus 24h: run cost/latency probes (4h window) to detect perf regressions vs staging baseline.
- T minus 4h: set maintenance page and slack status channel. Freeze merges to infra repo.
- T minus 2h: run final `terraform apply` for network & routing changes. Confirm state lock was released and get hash.
- T plus 0: begin traffic shift 5% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%. Wait at least one full request‑path latency percentile window (p95) and error budget check between jumps.

Automated gating checks (failures trigger rollback to staging routes):
- Error rate > 0.5% above baseline for 5m.
- P95 latency > 2x baseline for 10m.
- Deployment health (k8s/agent) has >1 crashloop in 5 minutes.
- CPU/GPU utilization > 90% sustained for 10 minutes without autoscale reaction.

Rollback strategy (fastest safe rollback):
1) Repoint traffic back to staging endpoints via DNS / load balancer rule with low TTL; confirm traffic drop on prod.
2) Revert TF network changes by applying saved pre‑cutover TF state or running `terraform apply` with the previous plan artifact.
3) If state corruption suspected, restore state from snapshot and coordinate with vendor support.
4) Postmortem within 24 hours, include recorded metrics + plan artifacts.

Post‑cutover validation suite (72h):
- Golden prompts: run canonical prompt set and compute delta on topk distribution and token costs.
- Data plane throughput test: sustained RPS for 30m at target QPS.
- Cache hit rate: confirm prefix/KV cache hit rate within expected band.
- Cost metrics: token cost per 1M tokens vs baseline.
- Audit logs: ensure KMS/HSM audit events show no unexpected denials.

Testing matrix (minimal recommended):
- Component: Network path — Test: traceroute + packet capture — Pass: no >30ms jump at any hop for prod region
- Component: Auth (mTLS) — Test: mutual TLS handshake from agent -> control plane — Pass: handshake completes w/ expected CN
- Component: Model load — Test: load standard LLM variant to GPU and run warmup sequence — Pass: model32768 load time <= baseline+10%
- Component: Observability — Test: trace end-to-end with a request id — Pass: request contains token-level cost in the trace

Runbook snippets (copy/paste tools):
- Snapshot state: `terraform state pull > /tmp/tfstate-prod-$(date +%F).json`
- Lock release check: `curl -s $TF_LOCK_API | jq .locks` (customer-specific)
- Partial plan: `terraform plan -target=module.network -out plan.network`
- Apply plan with approval: `terraform apply plan.network`

Known gotchas we hit in prior cutovers (notes to self):
- Ratelimit config applied in staging but not carried to prod due to missing var override — caused silent 429s. Fix: include explicit `rate_limit` var in prod variable set and put a sentinel test in preflight.
- KMS key alias mismatch: alias in staging pointed to key rotation schedule; prod alias pointed to new key. We added a key alias verification script that runs as preflight.
- BGP flap during cutover: physical crossconnect had asymmetric routing for a short period. Mitigation: coordinate short BGP withdraw + reannounce window with netops and schedule at low customer traffic period.

Open questions / follow ups (action items):
- Decide standard TTL for temporary cutover DNS entries (ops prefers 60s; registrar limits might force 300s). Owner: Network infra.
- Create a small library of `terraform plan` artifact parsers we can run to auto-detect risk signals (e.g., >10% resource replacements). Owner: infra‑automation.

Appendix: minimal TF examples we used in the field (note: these are informal snippets)
- Explicit provider wiring in module block:
  module \"vpc\" {
    source = \"git::ssh://git@github.com/redwood-inference/terraform-modules.git//vpc?ref=refs/heads/prod-ready\"
    providers = { aws = aws.customer }
    # vars...
  }

- Breaking a big plan into targets:
  # CI runner config: run three plans sequentially
  terraform plan -target=module.vpc -out plan.vpc || exit 1
  terraform plan -target=module.crossconnect -out plan.cross || exit 1
  terraform plan -target=module.routing -out plan.route || exit 1

If anything here looks unfamiliar to a customer lead, call them early. Real cutovers succeed when customers and ops have the same mental model and a short, shared checklist.

Last updated notes: added TF provider alias guidance after INF-3124 investigation; updated run cadence to include 24–48h shadowing period per legal/privacy ask.

Unresolved: gather a small set of production postmortem examples to append as a gallery (ops: Ethan to collect).

---
(worksheet/working doc style — feel free to copy into a ticket or make a canonical checklist in Confluence)

Pre‑cutover checklist (must pass before any traffic shift):
- TF state snapshot: run `terraform state pull > tfstate-backup-YYYYMMDD` and upload to customer secure storage.
- State lock health: confirm no long locks in lock table and lock TTLs are sane.
- Secrets: verify KMS/HSM key access and rotation window with customer (test encrypt/decrypt).
- Certificates: edge certs + mTLS certs validated for CN/SAN with production hostnames.
- DNS prewiring: production DNS entries created with short TTL (60s) but set to 300s during maintenance if registrar limits.
- BGP/HA failover: if using physical crossconnect, confirm BGP session establishment and route advertisements with netops.
- Observability: ensure tracing hooks and token‑level cost metrics enabled for the prod namespace.
- Backout window and stakeholders on call: list of pager contacts + escalation tree.

Terraform quirks and recommended mitigations (field notes):
- Provider version skew: some customers pin provider versions in their enterprise catalog. When bumping provider in TF modules, include a migration commit that uses `terraform init -upgrade` in a sandbox and run `terraform plan` to detect provider-induced diffs.
- Provider alias collisions across modules: when modules use provider aliases, TF may create duplicate provider configs after module ref rework. Mitigation: add explicit `provider = aws.customer` in module blocks and include a short note in the module README.
- Lifecycle ignore_changes pitfalls: we rely on `ignore_changes` for ephemeral fields (e.g., ephemeral ip). Downside — drift detection hides unintended changes. Use `ignore_changes` sparingly and document where used.
- Large TF plans time out via our CI runner for big networking changes. Workaround: break plan into smaller scopes (`terraform plan -target=module.vpc`), or run plan on a larger runner with higher timeout.
- Remote state drift after manual changes: customers sometimes perform hand edits in console. Always run `terraform refresh` as part of preflight and capture a refresh diff artifact.
- State imports: importing resources with different IDs (e.g., cloud vendor vs on‑prem controller) can require manual mapping. Keep a mapping table in the ticket and capture `terraform state mv` commands.
- Locked resources in frequent apply loops: long‑running apply scripts (build loops) can leave locks. We recommend a tagging standard and an operator script that lists locks and TTLs; avoid automated retries that bypass TTL checks.

Cutover run steps (detailed):
- T minus 72h: create production TF workspace, copy variable set but do NOT apply. Run `terraform plan` and save artifact. Share plan with customer infra lead.
- T minus 48h: spin up prod control plane agents behind a shadowed router (traffic mirrored). Run smoke suite (auth, kv cache priming, model load).
- T minus 24h: run cost/latency probes (4h window) to detect perf regressions vs staging baseline.
- T minus 4h: set maintenance page and slack status channel. Freeze merges to infra repo.
- T minus 2h: run final `terraform apply` for network & routing changes. Confirm state lock was released and get hash.
- T plus 0: begin traffic shift 5% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%. Wait at least one full request‑path latency percentile window (p95) and error budget check between jumps.

Automated gating checks (failures trigger rollback to staging routes):
- Error rate > 0.5% above baseline for 5m.
- P95 latency > 2x baseline for 10m.
- Deployment health (k8s/agent) has >1 crashloop in 5 minutes.
- CPU/GPU utilization > 90% sustained for 10 minutes without autoscale reaction.

Rollback strategy (fastest safe rollback):
1) Repoint traffic back to staging endpoints via DNS / load balancer rule with low TTL; confirm traffic drop on prod.
2) Revert TF network changes by applying saved pre‑cutover TF state or running `terraform apply` with the previous plan artifact.
3) If state corruption suspected, restore state from snapshot and coordinate with vendor support.
4) Postmortem within 24 hours, include recorded metrics + plan artifacts.

Post‑cutover validation suite (72h):
- Golden prompts: run canonical prompt set and compute delta on topk distribution and token costs.
- Data plane throughput test: sustained RPS for 30m at target QPS.
- Cache hit rate: confirm prefix/KV cache hit rate within expected band.
- Cost metrics: token cost per 1M tokens vs baseline.
- Audit logs: ensure KMS/HSM audit events show no unexpected denials.

Testing matrix (minimal recommended):
- Component: Network path — Test: traceroute + packet capture — Pass: no >30ms jump at any hop for prod region
- Component: Auth (mTLS) — Test: mutual TLS handshake from agent -> control plane — Pass: handshake completes w/ expected CN
- Component: Model load — Test: load standard LLM variant to GPU and run warmup sequence — Pass: model32768 load time <= baseline+10%
- Component: Observability — Test: trace end-to-end with a request id — Pass: request contains token-level cost in the trace

Runbook snippets (copy/paste tools):
- Snapshot state: `terraform state pull > /tmp/tfstate-prod-$(date +%F).json`
- Lock release check: `curl -s $TF_LOCK_API | jq .locks` (customer-specific)
- Partial plan: `terraform plan -target=module.network -out plan.network`
- Apply plan with approval: `terraform apply plan.network`

Known gotchas we hit in prior cutovers (notes to self):
- Ratelimit config applied in staging but not carried to prod due to missing var override — caused silent 429s. Fix: include explicit `rate_limit` var in prod variable set and put a sentinel test in preflight.
- KMS key alias mismatch: alias in staging pointed to key rotation schedule; prod alias pointed to new key. We added a key alias verification script that runs as preflight.
- BGP flap during cutover: physical crossconnect had asymmetric routing for a short period. Mitigation: coordinate short BGP withdraw + reannounce window with netops and schedule at low customer traffic period.

Open questions / follow ups (action items):
- Decide standard TTL for temporary cutover DNS entries (ops prefers 60s; registrar limits might force 300s). Owner: Network infra.
- Create a small library of `terraform plan` artifact parsers we can run to auto-detect risk signals (e.g., >10% resource replacements). Owner: infra‑automation.

Appendix: minimal TF examples we used in the field (note: these are informal snippets)
- Explicit provider wiring in module block:
  module \"vpc\" {
    source = \"git::ssh://git@github.com/redwood-inference/terraform-modules.git//vpc?ref=refs/heads/prod-ready\"
    providers = { aws = aws.customer }
    # vars...
  }

- Breaking a big plan into targets:
  # CI runner config: run three plans sequentially
  terraform plan -target=module.vpc -out plan.vpc || exit 1
  terraform plan -target=module.crossconnect -out plan.cross || exit 1
  terraform plan -target=module.routing -out plan.route || exit 1

If anything here looks unfamiliar to a customer lead, call them early. Real cutovers succeed when customers and ops have the same mental model and a short, shared checklist.

Last updated notes: added TF provider alias guidance after INF-3124 investigation; updated run cadence to include 24–48h shadowing period per legal/privacy ask.

Unresolved: gather a small set of production postmortem examples to append as a gallery (ops: Ethan to collect).

---
(worksheet/working doc style — feel free to copy into a ticket or make a canonical checklist in Confluence)

title: QBR KPI blueprint — translating infra metrics into renewal signals
preview:
Purpose:
This working draft maps raw infra metrics (latency percentiles, uptime, token cost, cache hit rate) to customer-facing KPI definitions and a renewal signal rubric. Goal is a single compact table teams can reference during QBR prep, renewal risk scoring, and escalation playbooks.

Audience:
- CSMs preparing QBRs
- Solutions and SREs who supply metric extracts
- Rev/AMs who want a quantified renewal signal tied to operational observations

High-level approach (notes to self):
- Prefer percentile + SLO gap over simple averages (P95/P99 tell the story for tail latency)
- Use rolling 30d windows for renewal signals; use 7d windows for incident snapshots in review decks
- Convert metric deltas into a 0-100 renewal-risk score (see translation matrix)
- Maintain a minimal set of ownerable thresholds so CS team can act without engineering rework

KPI definitions (canonical) and calculation snippets:
(Use these as canonical copy in QBR slides and SQL extracts)

1) Latency (text-gen token response time)
- Definition: time from request accepted by Redwood API to first token emitted (server-side), and time to final token for full-response latency.
- Metric names: api.first_token_latency_ms, api.last_token_latency_ms
- Measurement: P50 / P95 / P99 over rolling window; also track % of requests above 1s / 3s thresholds per customer.
- Suggested SLOs (example customer baseline): P95 < 500ms, P99 < 1200ms for short prompts; for long streaming jobs use per-token pacing.
- Sample SQL (psuedocode):
  SELECT
    percentile_cont(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY first_token_ms) AS p95_first_token
  FROM events
  WHERE customer_id = :cid AND timestamp >= now() - interval '30 days';

2) Uptime / Availability
- Definition: percentage of 1-minute buckets in which customer requests succeeded (2xx or 3xx) vs total attempts. Exclude planned maintenance windows noted in maintenance history.
- Metric names: api.request_success_rate_1m
- Measurement: rolling 30d availability percentage; also count of outage minutes (>= 5 consecutive failing minutes = outage incident).
- Example threshold: >= 99.9% (Bronze), 99.95% (Silver), 99.99% (Gold SLA).
- Note: correlate availability drops with region and model variant.

3) Cost per token (unit econ)
- Definition: aggregated infra cost divided by tokens served for a customer or route over evaluation window. Should include GPU runtime cost, model variant premium, and direct caching savings credits.
- Metric names: billing.estimated_infra_cost_usd, usage.tokens_generated
- Measurement formula (string): total_infra_cost_usd / total_tokens_generated.
- Practical sample: if month cost = $2,400 and tokens = 1.2B -> cost/token = $0.000002 = $0.000002 per token (show in USD per 1k tokens).
- Notes: show both gross cost/token and marginal cost saving from cache (see cache section).

4) Cache hit rate (prefix + kv cache effectiveness)
- Definition: fraction of requests (or request tokens) for which a cache entry prevented full model execution. We track both prefix-hit (response reused) and kv-cache-hit (internal reuse during generation).
- Metric names: cache.prefix_hit_ratio, cache.kv_hit_ratio
- Measurement: hits / requests over window; also conditional hit-rate for top 10 routes.
- Example threshold: > 30% prefix hit is strong for retrieval-heavy customers; kv-hit > 60% indicates good reuse for multi-turn chat.

Translation matrix: metric deviations -> renewal signal and sample CSM action
(Use the table below on QBR one-pager)

Metric deviation | Severity (renewal signal) | Suggested first-step rationale/action | Example slide callout
---|---:|---|---
P95 latency increased by > 2x vs baseline (30d) | High (score +30) | Check recent rollout/model-variant changes; run per-route P95; enable canary fallback to previous quantized variant | "P95 doubled after model upgrade on 2025-01-10; recommend rollback to v1.3"
P99 latency > 1200ms for >1% of requests | High (score +25) | Open infra ticket, propose temporary rate-limit or burst pool for customer | "Tail latency incidents impacted interactive workflows"
Availability drop > 0.5% (30d) or any outage > 30 min | Critical (score +40) | Pager + SRE postmortem; prioritize SLA credit discussion | "2 outages in last 30d (45m, 22m) -> elevated renewal risk"
Cost/token increases > 40% QoQ with no business justification | Medium-High (score +20) | Review model selection, batching, and quantization; propose Optimize session | "Unit cost up 58% due to shift to high-capacity model"
Cache hit rate decrease > 15 percentage points | Medium (score +15) | Audit prompt routing/keys, check TTLs and caching policy changes | "Cache TTL cut from 24h to 30m in Dec release -> cache effectiveness down"
Stable metrics inside thresholds | Low (score 0) | Standard QBR talking points: growth, usage, feature asks | "Healthy operational baseline"

Renewal signal scoring (simple):
- Compute sum of severity scores across active deviations in 30d window.
- Map to quick bands for CSMs: 0-15 = Low risk, 16-40 = Medium risk (action plan suggested), 41+ = High risk (eng escalation + SLA discussion).

Sample KPI table for QBR slide (copyable):
KPI | Definition | Window | KPIs to show on slide | Owner
---|---|---:|---|---
P95 latency | server-side first-token P95 (ms) | 30d | P50 / P95 / P99 + % requests > 1s | Solutions/SRE
Availability | % of 1m buckets with successful responses | 30d | rolling % + outage count & minutes | SRE/CS Ops
Cost per 1k tokens | infra cost in USD per 1k tokens | 30d | current / previous quarter % change | Finance/CS
Cache hit rate | prefix hit % and kv-hit % | 30d | overall hit rate + top 10 routes | Solutions

Playbook snippets (CS-facing)
- If P95 > baseline * 2: open support ticket with "latency-regression" tag; request per-route P95 breakdown; propose canary/fallback during QBR prep.
- If availability < SLA: escalate to SRE lead, request incident timeline + mitigation plan; prepare commercial remediation options for AM.
- If cost/token uptick: create an Optimize recommendation (batching, quantization, cheaper model variant) and a short ROI table.

Example ROI calculation (copyable in slide):
- Current month tokens: 1,200,000,000
- Current infra cost: $2,400 -> cost/1k tokens = (2400 / 1,200,000) = $0.002 per 1k tokens (double-check units when prepping slide)
- Proposed change reduces GPU time by 30% -> expected saving = 0.3 * infra_cost = $720/month -> annualized ~ $8.6k

Operational notes and caveats:
- Sampling bias: small customers can have noisy percentiles. Use min-request thresholds (e.g., >10k reqs/month) before surfacing hard recommendations.
- Correlation vs causation: cost/token up can be due to new product features (longer completions) not infra regressions. Always surface product changes in QBR narrative.
- Cache metrics require consistent keys and documented TTLs; if a customer uses variable prompt prefixes, prefix-hit is not stable.

Open questions / TODOs:
- [ ] Agree canonical per-customer min volume thresholds for percentile validity (ask analytics).
- [ ] Build a simple dashboard widget that computes renewal score automatically (product request). Jira CS-782 opened.
- [ ] Standardize cost calculations with finance team (billing.estimated vs billable).

Appendix: quick SQL/PromQL cheats
- P95 latency (PromQL): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{customer_id=~"$CID"}[30d])) by (le))
- Cache hit rate (SQL): SELECT 100.0 * SUM(case when cache_hit=true then 1 else 0 end)/COUNT(1) as hit_pct FROM cache_events WHERE customer_id = :cid AND ts >= now() - interval '30 days';

Notes to CSMs (one-paragraph copy for QBR):
"Operational health: We track P95/P99 latency, uptime, cost/unit, and cache effectiveness as the four signals that most strongly correlate with retention risk. In this QBR we recommend highlighting any >30d regressions, showing mitigation steps, and committing a 7d stabilization plan. For cost upticks include a short Optimize recommendation and expected ROI."

Revision history:
- 2024-11-05: initial draft (Priya)
- 2024-12-12: added translation matrix (Ethan)
- 2025-01-31: included ROI calc and PromQL example (Sofia)
- 2025-02-12: moved to in_review after SRE feedback (Ben)

End of doc.

title: Regional handoff protocol and variant degradation checklist
preview:
Purpose and context:

This doc captures an operationally-oriented protocol and checklist for handing requests across regions when capacity is constrained, and for progressively degrading model fidelity with minimal user impact. It is intended for platform engineers implementing control-plane routing logic and SREs validating runbooks during incident drills.

High-level goals:
- Keep surface-level API availability at or above the SLA (99.9% availability for hosted tier) by shifting requests across regions and model variants when local capacity or latency envelopes are compromised.
- Minimize quality regressions by preferring smaller, controlled degradations (e.g., shorter context, quantized variant, lower compute family) over wholesale failures.
- Make routing decisions observable, reversible, and auditable via control-plane traces and rollout metadata.

Core concepts (compact):
- Fast lanes vs safe lanes: fast lanes prioritize latency (shorter queues, smaller batches) and favor smaller model families; safe lanes prioritize quality and cost predictability. Control plane marks each route with a lane tag.
- Spillover: automated request redirection across AZs/regions when local load exceeds capacity thresholds (see thresholds section). Spillover is compositionally applied after tiered-shedding.
- Spillback: when upstream region rejects due to capacity, the control plane re-evaluates next candidate regions rather than immediate failure. A bounded retry budget prevents amplification.
- Variant ladder: a prioritized list of compatible model variants for a given request profile (e.g., gpt-large-fp16 -> gpt-medium-q4 -> gpt-small-q4.1). Ladder entries annotate expected latency/cost/quality deltas.
- Control-plane policy object: route_policy {priority, lane, retry_budget, max_latency_ms, compatible_variants, fallback_regions} attached to each customer-route.

Decision thresholds (proposal):
- Soft-shed threshold (local CPU/GPU util): 85% moving average over 60s triggers first-level shedding (reduce per-request KV cache window, lower batch target).
- Tier-1 spillover threshold (latency p95 > 2x normal OR queue depth > 1000): enable cross-AZ spillover within same region.
- Tier-2 spillover threshold (regional capacity shortfall OR sustained p99 elevation > 90s): enable cross-region spillover to preserved fallback regions.
- Retry budget: 3 attempts max per request (original region + up to 2 failover regions), total client-visible latency budget enforced.

Routing policy examples (pseudo):
- policy: low-latency-chat (lane: fast, retry_budget:2, compatible_variants:[c-fp16, c-q4f], fallback_regions:[us-west-2, eu-west-1], max_latency_ms:1200)
- policy: high-quality-batch (lane: safe, retry_budget:0, compatible_variants:[hq-fp16], fallback_regions:[same-region-only], max_latency_ms:5000)

Model-variant handling and rules:
- Variants are grouped into families. A family maps to an equivalence class for tokenization/response shape. The control plane must only substitute within the same family to preserve behaviors that downstream features rely on (e.g., structured tool calls).
- Annotate variants with quality_score (0..1) from offline evals and production monitors. Variants with quality_score < 0.7 require a per-customer opt-in to be used as fallback.
- Kernel compatibility: control plane must consult a model-compatibility table (compiled by serving-runtime) before routing to a variant; incompatible variants are excluded even when capacity would otherwise allow them.

Caching and prefix considerations:
- Prefix/KV cache bypass: when switching to a smaller variant, the control plane will try to preserve KV cache hits by truncating or translating the KV keys where supported. If that fails, mark request as cold-start for monitoring.
- Opportunistic reuse: if serving-runtime exposes a shared KV store across variants (same family), reuse entries to avoid regen costs. Fallback logic must decrement cache-hit expectations accordingly.

Observability and SLOs to monitor (minimal set):
- Per-route error rate (5xx and 429) + per-variant error rate
- Cross-region round-trip latency + failed handoffs
- Quality regression signal: per-route BLEU/embedding-sim drift vs golden prompts (daily) and token-level perplexity delta (real-time sampled).
- Fallback frequency: percent of requests that hit a variant-ladder fallback or cross-region spillover
- Retry amplification: requests that cause >1 extra request inside the system due to handoff loops

Runbook / checklist for triggering manual intervention:
- Situation: region us-east-1 sustained p99 >SLO and automated spillover started
  1) Check control-plane event stream for recent policy flips and variant substitutions (query trace id).
  2) Validate fallback regions' capacity (SRE dashboard) and confirm no cascading limits (network egress, ACLs).
  3) If fallback regions are saturated, enable conservative global-shed policy: apply headroom multiplier of 0.75 to batch sizes and enforce lane=fast for non-enterprise customers.
  4) Notify on-call and escalate to platform lead if regional spillover rate >10% for >15 minutes.
  5) Roll back any recent model rollouts that coincide with degradation (use rollout_id audit).

Operational knobs for graceful degradation (what to tune):
- Ladder depth: how many lower-quality variants to attempt before failing (default 2).
- Per-customer fallback opt-out flag: customers can opt out of cross-region spillover for data residency reasons.
- Canary gates: only allow variant substitution for a small percent (5%) of traffic initially; raise on green metrics.
- Latency budget: per-route TTL after handoff; if exceeded, return a structured error that indicates degraded mode rather than opaque 5xx.

Testing and verification matrix (what to simulate):
- Single-region GPU exhaustion: spin up load generator for 30m, validate spillover to AZ and region, assert no request loops, assert retry budget respected.
- KV cache translation failure: simulate variant family mismatch and confirm metric emitted and client-visible cold-start delta tracked.
- Rollout-induced regressions: run synthetic golden-prompt set at 10 QPS while toggling variant ladder entries; assert quality_score delta < 3% for canary group.

Open questions and design tradeoffs (actionable):
- Should the control plane prefer cross-region spillover or stronger intra-region degradation (more aggressive quantization) when both are possible? Tradeoff: latency vs data-residency and egress cost. Proposal: prefer intra-region degraded variant first for non-enterprise tenants.
- How to represent non-functional compatibility (tool-calling, streaming) in the variant metadata so substitution avoids breaking client contracts? Action: add boolean flags to variant manifests (supports-streaming, supports-tools) and expose fail-fast checks in routing.
- How large should the retry budget be to avoid amplifying load during cascading outages? Current conservative default is 2; consider lowering to 1 for bursty traffic patterns.

Checklist for merging policy changes (prerelease):
- Unit tests for policy evaluation logic (including edge cases).
- End-to-end staging run with synthetic load and golden prompts.
- Observability hooks: new metrics, dashboard tiles, and alerting rules created before rollout.
- Rollout plan with automatic rollback on quality or error thresholds.

Notes to self / loose reminders (informal):
- Add an event type in control-plane traces for \"variant-translation-failed\" with kv_key and original_variant fields.
- Coordinate with legal on opt-out semantics for cross-region spillover — ensure SLA language covers it.
- Short-term: implement a debug endpoint that simulates ladder traversal for a given request fingerprint.

Appendix (short):
- Recommended fallback region pairs (preserving timezones and egress): us-east-1 <-> us-west-2, eu-west-1 <-> eu-central-1, ap-southeast-1 <-> ap-northeast-1.
- Quick example audit line: {route_id: R-1234, policy:v2, attempted:[us-east-1/c-fp16, us-west-2/c-q4f], result: us-west-2/c-q4f, latency_ms:870, trace: T-987654}

If this doc looks good, next steps: 1) convert decision thresholds into config schema PR, 2) add model-compatibility API, 3) schedule incident drill with SRE and platform engineers to validate behavior.

Unresolved: assign owners for the model-compatibility table update (suggest Ethan) and for legal/regulatory review (suggest Sofia).
text:
Purpose and context:

This doc captures an operationally-oriented protocol and checklist for handing requests across regions when capacity is constrained, and for progressively degrading model fidelity with minimal user impact. It is intended for platform engineers implementing control-plane routing logic and SREs validating runbooks during incident drills.

High-level goals:
- Keep surface-level API availability at or above the SLA (99.9% availability for hosted tier) by shifting requests across regions and model variants when local capacity or latency envelopes are compromised.
- Minimize quality regressions by preferring smaller, controlled degradations (e.g., shorter context, quantized variant, lower compute family) over wholesale failures.
- Make routing decisions observable, reversible, and auditable via control-plane traces and rollout metadata.

Core concepts (compact):
- Fast lanes vs safe lanes: fast lanes prioritize latency (shorter queues, smaller batches) and favor smaller model families; safe lanes prioritize quality and cost predictability. Control plane marks each route with a lane tag.
- Spillover: automated request redirection across AZs/regions when local load exceeds capacity thresholds (see thresholds section). Spillover is compositionally applied after tiered-shedding.
- Spillback: when upstream region rejects due to capacity, the control plane re-evaluates next candidate regions rather than immediate failure. A bounded retry budget prevents amplification.
- Variant ladder: a prioritized list of compatible model variants for a given request profile (e.g., gpt-large-fp16 -> gpt-medium-q4 -> gpt-small-q4.1). Ladder entries annotate expected latency/cost/quality deltas.
- Control-plane policy object: route_policy {priority, lane, retry_budget, max_latency_ms, compatible_variants, fallback_regions} attached to each customer-route.

Decision thresholds (proposal):
- Soft-shed threshold (local CPU/GPU util): 85% moving average over 60s triggers first-level shedding (reduce per-request KV cache window, lower batch target).
- Tier-1 spillover threshold (latency p95 > 2x normal OR queue depth > 1000): enable cross-AZ spillover within same region.
- Tier-2 spillover threshold (regional capacity shortfall OR sustained p99 elevation > 90s): enable cross-region spillover to preserved fallback regions.
- Retry budget: 3 attempts max per request (original region + up to 2 failover regions), total client-visible latency budget enforced.

Routing policy examples (pseudo):
- policy: low-latency-chat (lane: fast, retry_budget:2, compatible_variants:[c-fp16, c-q4f], fallback_regions:[us-west-2, eu-west-1], max_latency_ms:1200)
- policy: high-quality-batch (lane: safe, retry_budget:0, compatible_variants:[hq-fp16], fallback_regions:[same-region-only], max_latency_ms:5000)

Model-variant handling and rules:
- Variants are grouped into families. A family maps to an equivalence class for tokenization/response shape. The control plane must only substitute within the same family to preserve behaviors that downstream features rely on (e.g., structured tool calls).
- Annotate variants with quality_score (0..1) from offline evals and production monitors. Variants with quality_score < 0.7 require a per-customer opt-in to be used as fallback.
- Kernel compatibility: control plane must consult a model-compatibility table (compiled by serving-runtime) before routing to a variant; incompatible variants are excluded even when capacity would otherwise allow them.

Caching and prefix considerations:
- Prefix/KV cache bypass: when switching to a smaller variant, the control plane will try to preserve KV cache hits by truncating or translating the KV keys where supported. If that fails, mark request as cold-start for monitoring.
- Opportunistic reuse: if serving-runtime exposes a shared KV store across variants (same family), reuse entries to avoid regen costs. Fallback logic must decrement cache-hit expectations accordingly.

Observability and SLOs to monitor (minimal set):
- Per-route error rate (5xx and 429) + per-variant error rate
- Cross-region round-trip latency + failed handoffs
- Quality regression signal: per-route BLEU/embedding-sim drift vs golden prompts (daily) and token-level perplexity delta (real-time sampled).
- Fallback frequency: percent of requests that hit a variant-ladder fallback or cross-region spillover
- Retry amplification: requests that cause >1 extra request inside the system due to handoff loops

Runbook / checklist for triggering manual intervention:
- Situation: region us-east-1 sustained p99 >SLO and automated spillover started
  1) Check control-plane event stream for recent policy flips and variant substitutions (query trace id).
  2) Validate fallback regions' capacity (SRE dashboard) and confirm no cascading limits (network egress, ACLs).
  3) If fallback regions are saturated, enable conservative global-shed policy: apply headroom multiplier of 0.75 to batch sizes and enforce lane=fast for non-enterprise customers.
  4) Notify on-call and escalate to platform lead if regional spillover rate >10% for >15 minutes.
  5) Roll back any recent model rollouts that coincide with degradation (use rollout_id audit).

Operational knobs for graceful degradation (what to tune):
- Ladder depth: how many lower-quality variants to attempt before failing (default 2).
- Per-customer fallback opt-out flag: customers can opt out of cross-region spillover for data residency reasons.
- Canary gates: only allow variant substitution for a small percent (5%) of traffic initially; raise on green metrics.
- Latency budget: per-route TTL after handoff; if exceeded, return a structured error that indicates degraded mode rather than opaque 5xx.

Testing and verification matrix (what to simulate):
- Single-region GPU exhaustion: spin up load generator for 30m, validate spillover to AZ and region, assert no request loops, assert retry budget respected.
- KV cache translation failure: simulate variant family mismatch and confirm metric emitted and client-visible cold-start delta tracked.
- Rollout-induced regressions: run synthetic golden-prompt set at 10 QPS while toggling variant ladder entries; assert quality_score delta < 3% for canary group.

Open questions and design tradeoffs (actionable):
- Should the control plane prefer cross-region spillover or stronger intra-region degradation (more aggressive quantization) when both are possible? Tradeoff: latency vs data-residency and egress cost. Proposal: prefer intra-region degraded variant first for non-enterprise tenants.
- How to represent non-functional compatibility (tool-calling, streaming) in the variant metadata so substitution avoids breaking client contracts? Action: add boolean flags to variant manifests (supports-streaming, supports-tools) and expose fail-fast checks in routing.
- How large should the retry budget be to avoid amplifying load during cascading outages? Current conservative default is 2; consider lowering to 1 for bursty traffic patterns.

Checklist for merging policy changes (prerelease):
- Unit tests for policy evaluation logic (including edge cases).
- End-to-end staging run with synthetic load and golden prompts.
- Observability hooks: new metrics, dashboard tiles, and alerting rules created before rollout.
- Rollout plan with automatic rollback on quality or error thresholds.

Notes to self / loose reminders (informal):
- Add an event type in control-plane traces for \"variant-translation-failed\" with kv_key and original_variant fields.
- Coordinate with legal on opt-out semantics for cross-region spillover — ensure SLA language covers it.
- Short-term: implement a debug endpoint that simulates ladder traversal for a given request fingerprint.

Appendix (short):
- Recommended fallback region pairs (preserving timezones and egress): us-east-1 <-> us-west-2, eu-west-1 <-> eu-central-1, ap-southeast-1 <-> ap-northeast-1.
- Quick example audit line: {route_id: R-1234, policy:v2, attempted:[us-east-1/c-fp16, us-west-2/c-q4f], result: us-west-2/c-q4f, latency_ms:870, trace: T-987654}

If this doc looks good, next steps: 1) convert decision thresholds into config schema PR, 2) add model-compatibility API, 3) schedule incident drill with SRE and platform engineers to validate behavior.

title: Slice profiling: coldstart vs warm-start pairwise labels and NDCG workbook
preview:
Purpose and scope

This is a working workbook for profiling reranker performance across query slices that highlight differences between coldstart and warm-start behavior. Primary goal: produce a reproducible pairwise-label + NDCG evaluation flow that surfaces regressions specific to cold/warm populations and gives reliable CIs for small slices. This doc is intentionally pragmatic — experiment notes, ad-hoc QC checks, and TODOs are included.

Background/context

Why coldstart vs warm-start? In production we observe two qualitative search/session patterns:
- coldstart: user issues first queries in a session (no cached KV/prefetch, query distribution more exploratory)
- warm-start: user issues follow-up queries or cursor/paginated interactions (benefits from cached context, user intent narrower)

Model variants may trade-off aggressive cross-document features (improve warm-start relevance) at the expense of early-session precision. We need granular slices and pairwise judgments to decide safe rollouts and smart fallbacks.

High-level evaluation approach

1) Sample generation: funnel-sampled queries from production logs spanning Oct-Dec 2024. Keep equal representation across devices and traffic buckets.
2) Construct pairs: for each query we produce top-K lists from baseline (prod) and candidate model, then generate candidate pairs (prod vs cand) for pairwise annotation.
3) Annotation protocol: binary preference label (A better | B better | Tie) with annotator notes and confidence score (1-3).
4) Metrics: compute NDCG@10 per slice and per query; aggregate with bootstrap to derive 95% CI. Also compute pairwise win-rate and preference-lift.

Dataset & sampling details

- Source logs: query_stream_2024-10_to_2024-12 (funnel-sampled by session start).
- Sampling unit: session_id + query_index. We mark query as coldstart if query_index == 0 (first query in session). Warm-start is query_index >= 1.
- Target sample sizes: 8k coldstart queries, 8k warm-start queries (target parity to isolate variance).
- For each query sample: retrieve top-10 results from baseline model (v1-prod) and candidate (v2-alpha). Keep canonical doc ids and rerank scores.
- Pair generation policy: prefer complete list comparisons when lists differ significantly; otherwise sample item-level pairs to avoid combinatorial explosion. This workbook records both list-level preference tasks and item-level pairwise tasks.

Annotation instructions (annotator-facing summary)

- Task: Given Query Q and two result lists (A and B) highlight which list better satisfies user intent. If identical, choose Tie. If both lists have strengths, mark \"Tie\" and add quick note.
- Time guideline: 20–35s per task for list-level; 8–12s per item-level pair.
- Preference rubric: relevancy > completeness > freshness > diversity. Provide an example bank: e.g., \"Query: 'budget laptop 13 inch' — prefer results with clear product specs and price range.\"
- Edge cases: Personalization signals should be ignored (annotate for general user). For ambiguous queries, prefer conservatism (Tie if unsure).
- QC checks: inject 6 golden tasks per 200 tasks; annotators with <85% golden accuracy in a batch flagged for review.

Label schema recorded in dataset:
- label: A | B | Tie
- confidence: 1 | 2 | 3 (3 = high)
- annotator_id
- task_type: list-level | item-pair
- session_flag: coldstart | warmstart

Metric calculations and bootstrap

- Per-query NDCG: compute DCG@k where relevance grades are derived from pairwise labels mapped back to item relevance scores using Bradley-Terry smoothing heuristic (see below).
- Why pairwise -> graded relevance: many tasks are pairwise; to compute NDCG we need graded relevance. We use an estimation step where each document gets a score proportional to its win-rate against other documents for the same query, regularized by a pseudo-count (alpha=1.0).
- DCG formula: DCG@k = sum_{i=1..k} (2^{rel_i}-1)/log2(i+1). Use rel_i = estimated_winrate * 3 (map to 0..3).
- Normalization: IDCG computed from ideal ordering by estimated rels. NDCG@10 computed per query; queries with fewer than 2 judged docs get excluded from NDCG aggregation (but still appear in pairwise summaries).
- Aggregation: compute mean NDCG across queries within each slice (cold/warm plus any slice intersection). Bootstrap: 2000 resamples of queries within slice to estimate 95% percentile CI. Report mean and (lower, upper).

Slice definitions and prioritization

Primary slices:
- coldstart (first query)
- warmstart (subsequent queries)
- head queries: top 10% by global frequency
- tail queries: bottom 50% by frequency (but with at least 5 occurrences in sample)
- long-query (tokens >= 8) vs short-query (tokens <= 3)
- commercial-intent vs informational (simple classifier tag)

We prioritize the intersection slices: coldstart+tail, warmstart+commercial, warmstart+long-query. Those are small; bootstrap CIs are necessary.

Preliminary results (pilot run, 2025-01-25) — do not treat as final

- Dataset: 2k cold / 2k warm pilot sample; 1.8k list-level tasks annotated.
- NDCG@10 (mean): baseline (v1-prod) cold: 0.472 (95% CI 0.460–0.485) | candidate (v2-alpha) cold: 0.459 (0.447–0.472) -> delta: -0.013 (stat borderline).
- Warm: baseline 0.512 (0.501–0.523) | candidate 0.536 (0.525–0.548) -> delta: +0.024 (significant).
- Pairwise win-rate: candidate wins 55% of warm tasks, 46% of cold tasks.

Interpretation notes:

- Candidate model improves follow-up interactions at expense of early-session relevance. Might be due to stronger context-dependent signals learned during fine-tuning.
- Warm improvement is large and consistent — candidate may be preferable for users with persistent sessions but risky for first-touch experiences.

Operational decisions under consideration

1) Rollout strategy: Canary to dedicated warm-path only (route queries with session_index >= 1 to candidate) and keep coldstart on baseline. Pros: preserves first-touch experience. Cons: routing complexity and UX parity.
2) Model interpolation: weighted blending of scores with larger weight for baseline at position 0. Simpler to implement but needs tuning.
3) Fallback policy: detect low-confidence candidate output and fallback to baseline for top-N.

Open questions and TODOs

- Improve pairwise->graded mapping: current BT smoothing alpha=1.0 is heuristic. Run sensitivity analysis for alpha in [0.1,1,5].
- Expand sample to full 8k/8k and rerun bootstrap with 2000 resamples.
- Add time-to-first-byte / latency-sensitivity slice to check UX tradeoffs.
- Prepare a small user study for top 5% of queries where candidate loses coldstart to validate real-user impact.

Annotation and QC artifacts to attach (next steps)

- Create consolidated annotation manifest (CSV) with golden task flags.
- Run inter-annotator agreement (Kripp alpha) per slice — aim for 0.65+.
- Build quick dashboard: NDCG delta plots, pairwise-win heatmap by slice, per-query waterfall for top 200 regression queries.

Notes to self (Priya)

- Check that session_index computed in ETL matches product session boundary definition (there's a ticket).
- Link the regression gating criteria doc and update CAL (canary acceptance levels) if candidate is routed by session_index.
- Flag privacy-sensitive queries before sending to external annotators.

Appendix: quick algorithmic sketch for pairwise -> relevance mapping

1) For each query q, build directed win counters: wins[d] = number of pairwise judgments where doc d preferred. Losses analogous.
2) Estimate winrate_w(d) = (wins[d] + alpha*prior) / (wins[d] + losses[d] + alpha). Prior = 0.5 (neutral).
3) Map rel = round(winrate_w * 3, 3) to produce continuous graded relevance in [0,3].
4) Use rel for DCG/NDCG formulas.

Recent tickets and refs

- JIRA-1234: ETL: session_index mismatch (blocked until resolved)
- PR: github.com/redwood-inference/rerank-evals/pull/88 (pilot annotation loader)
- Confluence: ndcg-regression-harness (documentation of bootstrap scripts)

End of workbook.

Unresolved: validation plan for the UX impact threshold that should override small NDCG deltas when the loss is concentrated on coldstart head queries.

If you're reviewing: read the Pilot Results -> Interpretation -> Rollout strategy sections first. Any questions ping me on Slack @priya_n.

title: Observability-driven state repair and idempotent routing notes
preview:
Purpose and scope:

This doc collects experimental notes and decision heuristics for an approach we can trial that puts observability at the center of eventual consistency handling across the control plane and routing layer. Target audience: platform engineers working on routing, control-plane state, and multi-region resiliency.

Problem statement (short):
- We run a geo-distributed control plane with partial replicas (etcd + CRDB hybrid ideas) and local caches in front of regionally-deployed routers.
- Staleness and transient divergence are inevitable; we need a pragmatic, operationally-safe way to detect, repair, and gracefully degrade without inventing more synchronous cross-region coordination.

High-level idea: observability-driven state repair + idempotent routing.
- Use rich telemetry (per-request traces, divergence counters, cache-miss/validation events) to detect probable inconsistencies early.
- Drive corrective actions from the control plane as asynchronous repair flows (invalidate, rehydrate, anti-entropy) rather than global locks or syncs.
- Ensure routing operations are idempotent and carry a small reconciliation token so retries / backfills are safe.

Key principles (engineering constraints):
1) Fast reads, eventual convergence: prefer local reads for latency SLOs, but surface divergence scores to product owners and automated repair agents.
2) Observability-first decisions: metric thresholds and anomaly detectors decide between no-op, soft-repair (cache refresh), or hard-repair (write-through to majority store + schedule read-repair).
3) Bounded state machine for repairs: every repair flows through a deterministic sequence so operators can reason about retries and failover behavior.
4) Fail-open for routing: if repair would cause unacceptable latency, route using best-effort local state and attach a freshness flag to responses.

Components and responsibilities:
- Router sidecar (local): maintains a read-through cache, exposes cache diagnostic metrics, and tags responses with freshness metadata. Supports idempotent apply endpoints (operationId + payload).
- Observability pipeline: consumes traces, cache-change events, and control-plane write logs. Runs lightweight heuristics: divergence score, hot-key staleness, and region-lagging indicators.
- Repair coordinator (control plane async worker): subscribes to detectors, executes invalidation / refresh / repair actions against regional caches and a chosen canonical store. Logs repair attempts as events that observability can correlate.

Decision heuristics (examples):
- Divergence score > 0.6 on a key path for >5m and request-error rate rising -> trigger soft repair (cache invalidation for that key + immediate read-through on next request).
- Hot key with high QPS and frequent tail-latency spikes -> schedule proactive rehydrate from canonical store once per minute with jitter, avoiding global floods.
- Cross-region write observed with TTL < 30s cached locally -> mark as transient and prefer rehydrate-only path; do not block routing.

DB/store choices and how they fit the pattern:
- etcd (current primary for leases and small coordination blobs): great for strongly consistent leader election and short-lived locks. Not great for large object state or high write fanout across regions. Use it for authoritative lease/ownership tokens and versioned pointers, not for full route catalog.
- CockroachDB / CRDB (or equivalent geo-replicated SQL): better for moderately sized catalogs with geo-replication guarantees. Good candidate for canonical model versions and rollout metadata when we need strong cross-region durability with less operational complexity than custom sharded stores.
- S3/Blob store: for large model artifacts and opaque blobs referenced by the control-plane pointers stored in ETCD/CRDB. Repairs may re-fetch artifacts asynchronously.

Suggested hybrid mapping:
- Fast small control pointers -> etcd (versioned, short TTL where appropriate).
- Canonical route/catalog metadata -> CRDB (region-aware replicas).
- Ephemeral cache state -> local router sidecars + redis for intra-region fast reads.

Cache invalidation patterns (practical ops-driven set):
1) Event-driven invalidate: control-plane publishes topic events (kafka) for object updates. Router subscribes and invalidates local keys. Works well but subject to lost events in partitions — hence we need repair.
2) Observability-triggered backfill: detectors ask router to rehydrate by reading canonical store when divergence metrics exceed thresholds.
3) Lease-driven ownership flips: when ownership moves, new owner writes a versioned pointer into etcd; routers watching the pointer will either invalidate or start rehydrating. Use causal version numbers to avoid races.
4) Bloom-filter assisted selective invalidation for high-cardinality catalogs (don't invalidate whole prefix; compute a small bloom for changed set and push to routers).

Idempotency and safe retry design:
- Every routing decision that can mutate observable state (e.g., attach ephemeral session metadata, bump counters, cache-writes) must carry an operation_id and an explicit causal version (version:v42). Servers must be able to dedupe based on op_id and check version monotonicity.
- For rehydrate flows, use compare-and-swap on local cache entries with version check to ensure older rehydrates do not overwrite newer local writes.

Repair workflows (example sequences):
1) Soft rehydrate:
   - Detector flags key X stale.
   - Repair coordinator issues invalidate(X) to router sidecar (async, best-effort).
   - Next request for X reads through to canonical store and updates local cache with versioned entry.
   - Observability sees warming and marks repair success.
2) Hard repair (write-repair):
   - Detector sees divergent canonical versions (region A sees v9 while region B reads v12) and critical correctness threshold crossed (e.g., policy mismatch for routing).
   - Coordinator triggers a write-repair: attempts to write the authoritative version to canonical store via majority-safe write (CRDB tx) and signals routers to rehydrate.
   - If canonical store cannot be updated quickly, mark route degraded and apply fallback routing (e.g., route to alternate region model) while alerting SRE.
3) Anti-entropy background scan:
   - Periodic low-priority scan compares compacted local index summaries with canonical index and queues missing/older keys for rehydrate. Use sampling to keep load bounded.

Operational visibility: what we must surface in the Console
- Per-route freshness score (0..1) with last-validated timestamp.
- Repair-events timeline per key/prefix.
- Anti-entropy status and backlog metrics.
- Dedup/op-id rejection rate and last seen op-id per session.

Open questions and tradeoffs (to discuss):
- How aggressive do we make automatic hard write-repairs? Risk: accidental overwrite of a correct region state due to mis-detection. Conservative: prefer human-in-the-loop for critical route map changes.
- Eventual consistency SLAs: do we publish a defined freshness SLA (e.g., 95th percentile freshness < 10s for 80% of keys)? This shapes how much repair automation we tolerate.
- Cost bound for rehydrate at scale: proactive rehydrates for hot keys can burn IO; need throttling heuristics tied to billing tiers / dedicated capacity.
- Choosing canonical store: CRDB gives us SQL semantics and transactional guarantees, but operationally heavier. Can a lightweight Raft-based multi-region store (with region leaders) be sufficient?

Immediate experiments (next 8 weeks):
1) Implement detector prototype for key divergence using existing trace logs. Build a simple scoring function and surface top-100 divergent keys in the Console.
2) Add operation_id dedupe to router apply endpoints and emit rejection metrics.
3) Wire a repair coordinator that can issue invalidate + rehydrate on-demand for a single prefix. Run in staging across two regions and measure repair latency and false positive rate.
4) Run load test for hot-key rehydrate throttling to quantify IO/cost impact.

Notes to self / reminders:
- Keep event bus ordering semantics loose: rely on versioned pointers and idempotency to avoid requiring strict ordering.
- Correlate repair events with user-facing incidents to evolve thresholds.
- Document behaviour in runbook: when to disable auto write-repair, how to escalate, and how to query per-key repair history from observability.

Appendix: useful queries and signals (draft)
- Divergence query: compare last_seen_version(regionA, keyX) vs global_highest_version(keyX) over last 10m window -> ratio -> divergence score.
- Hot-key detection: combine qps, miss-rate, tail-latency.
- Rehydrate success: new_local_version == canonical_version within 2s of rehydrate call.

References and previous work:
- See JIRA-PLAT-879 for initial outage that motivated repair automation.
- Related: confluence:platform-state-contracts-v2 for versioning conventions.
- PR: control-plane/pull/432 implemented early op_id dedupe; review notes inline.

End.
text:
Purpose and scope:

This doc collects experimental notes and decision heuristics for an approach we can trial that puts observability at the center of eventual consistency handling across the control plane and routing layer. Target audience: platform engineers working on routing, control-plane state, and multi-region resiliency.

Problem statement (short):
- We run a geo-distributed control plane with partial replicas (etcd + CRDB hybrid ideas) and local caches in front of regionally-deployed routers.
- Staleness and transient divergence are inevitable; we need a pragmatic, operationally-safe way to detect, repair, and gracefully degrade without inventing more synchronous cross-region coordination.

High-level idea: observability-driven state repair + idempotent routing.
- Use rich telemetry (per-request traces, divergence counters, cache-miss/validation events) to detect probable inconsistencies early.
- Drive corrective actions from the control plane as asynchronous repair flows (invalidate, rehydrate, anti-entropy) rather than global locks or syncs.
- Ensure routing operations are idempotent and carry a small reconciliation token so retries / backfills are safe.

Key principles (engineering constraints):
1) Fast reads, eventual convergence: prefer local reads for latency SLOs, but surface divergence scores to product owners and automated repair agents.
2) Observability-first decisions: metric thresholds and anomaly detectors decide between no-op, soft-repair (cache refresh), or hard-repair (write-through to majority store + schedule read-repair).
3) Bounded state machine for repairs: every repair flows through a deterministic sequence so operators can reason about retries and failover behavior.
4) Fail-open for routing: if repair would cause unacceptable latency, route using best-effort local state and attach a freshness flag to responses.

Components and responsibilities:
- Router sidecar (local): maintains a read-through cache, exposes cache diagnostic metrics, and tags responses with freshness metadata. Supports idempotent apply endpoints (operationId + payload).
- Observability pipeline: consumes traces, cache-change events, and control-plane write logs. Runs lightweight heuristics: divergence score, hot-key staleness, and region-lagging indicators.
- Repair coordinator (control plane async worker): subscribes to detectors, executes invalidation / refresh / repair actions against regional caches and a chosen canonical store. Logs repair attempts as events that observability can correlate.

Decision heuristics (examples):
- Divergence score > 0.6 on a key path for >5m and request-error rate rising -> trigger soft repair (cache invalidation for that key + immediate read-through on next request).
- Hot key with high QPS and frequent tail-latency spikes -> schedule proactive rehydrate from canonical store once per minute with jitter, avoiding global floods.
- Cross-region write observed with TTL < 30s cached locally -> mark as transient and prefer rehydrate-only path; do not block routing.

DB/store choices and how they fit the pattern:
- etcd (current primary for leases and small coordination blobs): great for strongly consistent leader election and short-lived locks. Not great for large object state or high write fanout across regions. Use it for authoritative lease/ownership tokens and versioned pointers, not for full route catalog.
- CockroachDB / CRDB (or equivalent geo-replicated SQL): better for moderately sized catalogs with geo-replication guarantees. Good candidate for canonical model versions and rollout metadata when we need strong cross-region durability with less operational complexity than custom sharded stores.
- S3/Blob store: for large model artifacts and opaque blobs referenced by the control-plane pointers stored in ETCD/CRDB. Repairs may re-fetch artifacts asynchronously.

Suggested hybrid mapping:
- Fast small control pointers -> etcd (versioned, short TTL where appropriate).
- Canonical route/catalog metadata -> CRDB (region-aware replicas).
- Ephemeral cache state -> local router sidecars + redis for intra-region fast reads.

Cache invalidation patterns (practical ops-driven set):

4) Bloom-filter assisted selective invalidation for high-cardinality catalogs (don't invalidate whole prefix; compute a small bloom for changed set and push to routers).

Idempotency and safe retry design:
- Every routing decision that can mutate observable state (e.g., attach ephemeral session metadata, bump counters, cache-writes) must carry an operation_id and an explicit causal version (version:v42). Servers must be able to dedupe based on op_id and check version monotonicity.

   - Observability sees warming and marks repair success.

   - If canonical store cannot be updated quickly, mark route degraded and apply fallback routing (e.g., route to alternate region model) while alerting SRE.
